title: K12Orgs
text:

The one metric that wasn’t included when I joined the enrollment data with the organizational data is the number of years they attended a school in the Southwest region.


enrollment.original <-read_csv("~/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/CRPD 2 (angelique.workman@state.mn.us)/K12Enrollment.csv")  %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID),
         K12OrganizationID = as.integer(K12OrganizationID)) %>%
  drop_na(PersonID)

SiteCountyCode <- read_csv("~/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/SiteCountyCodes.csv")

k12.org.original <- read_csv('/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/CRPD 2 (angelique.workman@state.mn.us)/K12Organization_cleaned.csv') %>%
  distinct(K12OrganizationID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  drop_na(K12OrganizationID) %>%
  mutate(K12OrganizationID = as.integer(K12OrganizationID)) %>%
  group_by(NCESID) %>%
  fill(SiteCountyCode, .direction = "downup") %>%
  ungroup()
  

k12.org.master <- k12.org.original %>%
  left_join(SiteCountyCode[,c(1,2)], by = c("SiteCountyCode" = "Code")) %>%
  drop_na(SiteCountyCode) %>%
  rename(county.name = `Code definition`) %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name) %>%
  mutate(county.name = str_replace(county.name, " County", ""),
         county.name = str_replace(county.name, "Saint Louis", "St. Louis")) %>%
  filter(county.name != "Unknown") %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name) %>%
  left_join(counties.regions.1, by = c("county.name" = "Name")) %>%
  select(-Dem_RUCA, -mif, -edr.simple)

enrollment.org.ne <- enrollment.original %>%
  left_join(k12.org.master, by = "K12OrganizationID") %>%
  filter(project.pr == "Southwest")

n.years <- original %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, K12OrganizationID) %>%
  rename(grad.org.id = K12OrganizationID) %>%
  left_join(enrollment.original[,c(1,2,3,4)], by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.org.original[,c(1,2,3,7)], by = "K12OrganizationID") %>%
  mutate(year = str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4)) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, year, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarize(n.years.attended = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

master <- original %>%
  left_join(n.years, by = "PersonID")

kable(head(master))

title: K12Orgs
text:
Summary of high school location
Lets breakdown the graduates by which RUCA category their high school was located.
Nearly 71% of the observations graduated from a county categorized as “town/rural mix”, followed by 15.1% that graduated from a high school in urban/town/rural mix counties and 14% from entirely rural counties.








Below is a map with the percentages of graduates graduating from high schools located within each county.

title: K12Orgs
text:
Summary of student demographics
The following analysis looks to see if there are any statistical differences due to rural-ness and geographic location of the high school for the following graduate demographics;

Were eligible for free or reduced lunch at some point in the dataset
Are Female
Have been homeless at some point in the dataset
Had limited english proficiency at some point in the dataset
Had non english speakders in home at some point in the dataset
Participated in a PSEO course at some point in the dataset.

The cross-tabs below indicate that there are 4 demographic variables that have proportions statistically different across RUCA categories. Each of the variables are indicated with an asterisk.

Free or reduced lunch: the more rural a district is, the higher the percentage of graduates that qualified for free or reduced lunch.
Homeless: as a school district becomes more urban, the percentage of it’s graduates indicating they experienced homelessness at some point increases significantly.
Limited English: the entirely rural districts had a slightly higher percentage of graduates with limited English.
Non-English in home: town/rural mix districts had a significantly higher percentage of graduates with non-English speakers in the home.
PSEO: a significantly lower percentage of graduates from entirely rural districts participated in a PSEO program.


RUCA Free or Reduced LunchRUCA FemaleRUCA HomelessRUCA Limited EnglishRUCA non-english in homeRUCA PSEO


Although 36.8% of the total number of graduates in the dataset receive free or reduced lunch, the more rural school districts had a higher percentage - 45.1% for entirely rural and 40.1% for town/rural mix schools.


CrossTable(master$Dem_Desc, master$economic.status, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)


 
   Cell Contents
|-------------------------|
|                       N |
|              Expected N |
|           N / Row Total |
|-------------------------|

 
Total Observations in Table:  54063 

 
                     | master$economic.status 
     master$Dem_Desc |         0 |         1 | Row Total | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Entirely rural |      5181 |      2476 |      7657 | 
                     |  4815.598 |  2841.402 |           | 
                     |     0.677 |     0.323 |     0.142 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Town/rural mix |     22681 |     15559 |     38240 | 
                     | 24049.687 | 14190.313 |           | 
                     |     0.593 |     0.407 |     0.707 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
Urban/town/rural mix |      6139 |      2027 |      8166 | 
                     |  5135.715 |  3030.285 |           | 
                     |     0.752 |     0.248 |     0.151 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
        Column Total |     34001 |     20062 |     54063 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|

 
Statistics for All Table Factors


Pearson's Chi-squared test 
------------------------------------------------------------
Chi^2 =  812.7926     d.f. =  2     p =  3.19396e-177 


 




k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca <- master %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  summarise(n = n(),
           free.lunch = sum(economic.status)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pct = free.lunch / n,
         total.pct = sum(free.lunch) / sum(n),
         data_id = as.character(seq(n()))) 

k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(n, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates received free or reduced lunch: ", comma(free.lunch, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 3,
           y = .4,
           label = "All graduates\npercent, 36.8%",
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates receiving free or reduced lunch")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16))

  girafe(ggobj = k12.dem.free.lunch.ruca.plot) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))








No significance.


CrossTable(master$Dem_Desc, master$Gender, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)


 
   Cell Contents
|-------------------------|
|                       N |
|              Expected N |
|           N / Row Total |
|-------------------------|

 
Total Observations in Table:  54063 

 
                     | master$Gender 
     master$Dem_Desc |         F |         M | Row Total | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Entirely rural |      3834 |      3823 |      7657 | 
                     |  3771.069 |  3885.931 |           | 
                     |     0.501 |     0.499 |     0.142 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Town/rural mix |     18739 |     19501 |     38240 | 
                     | 18833.181 | 19406.819 |           | 
                     |     0.490 |     0.510 |     0.707 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
Urban/town/rural mix |      4053 |      4113 |      8166 | 
                     |  4021.751 |  4144.249 |           | 
                     |     0.496 |     0.504 |     0.151 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
        Column Total |     26626 |     27437 |     54063 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|

 
Statistics for All Table Factors


Pearson's Chi-squared test 
------------------------------------------------------------
Chi^2 =  3.475817     d.f. =  2     p =  0.1758879 


 





As a district becomes more urban, the percentage of it’s graduates indicating it experienced homelessness increases significantly.


CrossTable(master$Dem_Desc, master$HomelessIndicator, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)


 
   Cell Contents
|-------------------------|
|                       N |
|              Expected N |
|           N / Row Total |
|-------------------------|

 
Total Observations in Table:  54063 

 
                     | master$HomelessIndicator 
     master$Dem_Desc |         N |         Y | Row Total | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Entirely rural |      7632 |        25 |      7657 | 
                     |  7619.751 |    37.249 |           | 
                     |     0.997 |     0.003 |     0.142 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Town/rural mix |     38038 |       202 |     38240 | 
                     | 38053.974 |   186.026 |           | 
                     |     0.995 |     0.005 |     0.707 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
Urban/town/rural mix |      8130 |        36 |      8166 | 
                     |  8126.275 |    39.725 |           | 
                     |     0.996 |     0.004 |     0.151 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
        Column Total |     53800 |       263 |     54063 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|

 
Statistics for All Table Factors


Pearson's Chi-squared test 
------------------------------------------------------------
Chi^2 =  5.777065     d.f. =  2     p =  0.05565783 


 



k12.dem.homeless.ruca <- master %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc, HomelessIndicator) %>%
  summarise(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(total.pct = sum(n[HomelessIndicator == "Y"]) / sum(n),
         graduates = sum(n)) %>%
  filter(HomelessIndicator == "Y") %>%
  mutate(total.homeless = sum(n),
         data_id = seq(n()))

  
k12.dem.homeless.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.dem.homeless.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.dem.homeless.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(graduates, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates homeless at some point: ", comma(total.homeless, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 1,
           y = k12.dem.homeless.ruca$total.pct - .001,
           label = paste("All graduates\npercent, ", percent(k12.dem.homeless.ruca$total.pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""),
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates homeless")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16))

  girafe(ggobj = k12.dem.homeless.ruca.plot) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))








The cross-tabs indicate a relationship. The chart shows that our .3% of entirely rural graduates were tagged as having Limited English which is significantly higher than town/rural mix (.1%).


CrossTable(master$Dem_Desc, master$LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)

                     |  7432.090 |   224.910 |           |

---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Town/rural mix |     36842 |      1398 |     38240 |

---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
Urban/town/rural mix |      8087 |        79 |      8166 |

Chi^2 =  239.8332     d.f. =  2     p =  8.334709e-53 


 



k12.dem.limited.english.ruca <- master %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc, LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator) %>%
  summarise(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(total.pct = sum(n[LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator == "Y"]) / sum(n),
         graduates = sum(n)) %>%
  filter(LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator == "Y") %>%
  mutate(LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator.total = sum(n),
         data_id = seq(n()))

  
k12.dem.limited.english.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.dem.limited.english.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.dem.limited.english.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(graduates, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates homeless at some point: ", comma(LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator.total, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 2,
           y = k12.dem.limited.english.ruca$total.pct + .0005,
           label = paste("All graduates\npercent, ", percent(k12.dem.limited.english.ruca$total.pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""),
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates with Limited English")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16))

    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))








Okay, well this doesn’t make a ton of sense. In the previous chart it showed that town/rural mix graduates were significantly less likely to have limited english, while this shows that town/rural mix graduates have the highest percentage of people that had non-english speakers at home.

k12.non.english.home.ruca <- master %>%

            non.english = sum(non.english.home)) %>%

           x = 1,
           y = k12.non.english.home.ruca$total.pct + .001,

    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),

Although 38.4% of all graduates participating in PSEO, there was a significantly lower percentage in entirely rural schools (30%).


CrossTable(master$Dem_Desc, master$pseo.participant, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)


 
   Cell Contents
|-------------------------|
|                       N |
|              Expected N |
|           N / Row Total |
|-------------------------|

 
Total Observations in Table:  43776 

 
                     | master$pseo.participant 
     master$Dem_Desc |         0 |         1 | Row Total | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Entirely rural |      3557 |      2428 |      5985 | 
                     |  3512.578 |  2472.422 |           | 
                     |     0.594 |     0.406 |     0.137 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
      Town/rural mix |     18642 |     12482 |     31124 | 
                     | 18266.580 | 12857.420 |           | 
                     |     0.599 |     0.401 |     0.711 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
Urban/town/rural mix |      3493 |      3174 |      6667 | 
                     |  3912.842 |  2754.158 |           | 
                     |     0.524 |     0.476 |     0.152 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
        Column Total |     25692 |     18084 |     43776 | 
---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|

 
Statistics for All Table Factors


Pearson's Chi-squared test 
------------------------------------------------------------
Chi^2 =  129.0862     d.f. =  2     p =  9.317381e-29 


 




k12.pseo.ruca<- master %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc, pseo.participant) %>%
  summarise(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(total.graduates = sum(n)) %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n),
         total.graduates = sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pseo.participant = ifelse(is.na(pseo.participant), -1, pseo.participant),
         total.pct = sum(n[pseo.participant == 1]) / sum(n)) %>%
  filter(pseo.participant == 1) %>%
  mutate(data_id = seq(n()))

k12.pseo.ruca.plot <- ggplot(k12.pseo.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = Dem_Desc)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = k12.pseo.ruca$total.pct, color = "black") +
  geom_col_interactive(size = 5, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(Dem_Desc, "\nTotal graduates: ", comma(total.graduates, accuracy = 1), "\nGraduates participated in PSEO: ", comma(n, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of total graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  annotate(geom = "text",
           x = 1,
           y = k12.pseo.ruca$total.pct,
           label = paste("All graduates\npercent, ", percent(k12.pseo.ruca$total.pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""),
           size = 5) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", fill = "", title = "Percent of graduates participated in PSEO")+
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_line+
  scale_fill_manual(values= color.ruca,
                     guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16))

  girafe(ggobj = k12.pseo.ruca.plot) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

The purpose of multiple correspondence analysis is to examine any patterns among the independent variables with the goal of eliminating variables that are redundant - essentially two variables that are explaining the same thing. This type of analysis is not meant to confirm any predictions, but rather explore the data and make decisions on what data should be included in the prediction model.

non.english.home






As expected, we have 41,580 individuals in the dataset.


MCA analysis


Eigenvalues - Demographic Variables
Overview The MCA on demographic variables produced 9 dimensions, collectively explaining 100% of the variance in the data.
Dimension Retention Using the average eigenvalue threshold (1/k = 0.167), Dimensions 1-5 are recommended for retention, collectively explaining 74.6% of the total variance. Dimension 1 is clearly the strongest, explaining 27.8% of variance, with a notable drop-off to subsequent dimensions which each explain approximately 11%.
Notes The relatively even spread of variance across Dimensions 2-5 suggests that demographic variation in this dataset is multidimensional, with no single dominant second axis of variation.


demographics.mca <- FactoMineR::MCA(demographics, graph = FALSE)

eig.val <- get_eigenvalue(demographics.mca)

eig.val

      eigenvalue variance.percent cumulative.variance.percent
Dim.1 0.41742752        27.828501                    27.82850
Dim.2 0.19859237        13.239491                    41.06799
Dim.3 0.17058027        11.372018                    52.44001
Dim.4 0.16700655        11.133770                    63.57378
Dim.5 0.16603845        11.069230                    74.64301
Dim.6 0.14925819         9.950546                    84.59356
Dim.7 0.12242458         8.161638                    92.75519
Dim.8 0.07143907         4.762604                    97.51780
Dim.9 0.03723301         2.482201                   100.00000




COS2 - Demographic Variables
Overview COS2 measures the quality of representation of each variable category across the five retained dimensions. Values closer to 1 indicate that a category is well represented in the retained dimensional space.
Well Represented Categories Race/ethnicity categories are the best represented across the five dimensions. Black (0.970), Asian/PI (0.930), Hispanic (0.827), and AI (0.829) all show very strong representation, indicating these categories are well captured by the retained dimensions. The language variables non.english.home (0.823) and White (0.750) are also well represented.
Moderately Represented Categories SpecialEdStatus (0.661), english.learner (0.647), and Gender (0.611) show moderate representation, suggesting some variation in these variables extends beyond the five retained dimensions.
Weakest Represented Categories economic.status is the weakest represented variable at 0.437, suggesting that economic variation among students is not well captured by the five retained dimensions and may operate somewhat independently of other demographic factors.
Notes The expected symmetry between binary category pairs (e.g., economic.status_0 and economic.status_1) is present throughout, confirming the technical integrity of the MCA results.
<br>

table <- demographics.mca$var$cos2 %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`, `Dim 3`, `Dim 4`, `Dim 5`) %>%
  mutate(total = `Dim 1` + `Dim 2` + `Dim 3` + `Dim 4` + `Dim 5`) 
  
datatable(table) %>%
  formatCurrency(1:6, "", digits = 3)




fviz_cos2(demographics.mca, choice = "var", axes = 1:5)











Contributions - Demographic Variables
Overview Contributions measure how much each variable category drives each retained dimension. Categories with higher contributions are the primary drivers of that dimension’s axis of variation.
Dimension 1 - English Language Learner / Hispanic Axis Dimension 1 is primarily driven by non.english.home_1 (27.2%), english.learner_1 (22.7%), and Hispanic (17.1%), indicating this axis captures variation among students who speak a non-English language at home, are English language learners, and are of Hispanic ethnicity. These three categories together account for over 67% of Dimension 1.
Dimension 2 - Special Education / Gender Axis Dimension 2 is dominated by SpecialEdStatus_1 (41.6%), with secondary contributions from Gender (F: 11.4%, M: 11.1%), suggesting this axis captures variation among students with special education status alongside an independent gender gradient.
Dimension 3 - American Indian / Gender Axis Dimension 3 is primarily driven by AI (48.9%) with notable secondary contributions from Gender (F: 14.3%, M: 14.0%), suggesting this axis captures variation unique to American Indian students alongside a gender gradient.
Dimension 4 - Multi-Minority Race/Ethnicity Axis Dimension 4 is shared across Black (33.8%), Hispanic (26.7%), and Asian/PI (19.1%) and AI (18.6%), suggesting this dimension captures a broader pattern of variation among minority race/ethnicity groups.
Dimension 5 - Black / Asian/PI Axis Dimension 5 is primarily driven by Black (46.6%) and Asian/PI (41.5%), suggesting this dimension captures residual variation that distinguishes these two groups from other minority race/ethnicity categories.
Notes The removal of unknown race/ethnicity individuals has produced a notably cleaner dimensional structure, particularly for Dimensions 3-5 which now tell a more interpretable story of variation among specific minority race/ethnicity groups.


table <- demographics.mca$var$contrib %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(id = seq(n())) %>%
  select(id, `Dim 1`, `Dim 2`, `Dim 3`, `Dim 4`, `Dim 5`) %>%
  mutate(`Dim 1` = `Dim 1` / 100,
         `Dim 2` = `Dim 2` / 100,
         `Dim 3` = `Dim 3` / 100,
         `Dim 4` = `Dim 4` / 100,
         `Dim 5` = `Dim 5` / 100)

datatable(table) %>%
  formatPercentage(2:6, digits = 1)








Summary - Demographic Variables
Multiple Correspondence Analysis on the demographic variables produced five retained dimensions explaining 74.6% of the total variance. Dimension 1 captured an English language learner / Hispanic axis, driven by non.english.home, english.learner, and Hispanic ethnicity. Dimension 2 captured a special education / gender axis, Dimension 3 an American Indian / gender axis, and Dimensions 4 and 5 captured variation among minority race/ethnicity groups.
Overall, the MCA revealed that most demographic variables are capturing distinct and meaningful axes of variation, with the exception of some expected overlap between the language variables and Hispanic ethnicity. Given that this overlap is conceptually expected in a Southwest Minnesota context and that each variable retains some independent information, no demographic variables were eliminated following MCA.
Variables to eliminate:

None

We have 50,830 individuals in the dataset.


MCA analysis

Eigenvalues - High School Characteristics
Overview The MCA on high school characteristics variables produced 4 dimensions, collectively explaining 100% of the variance in the data.
Dimension Retention Using the average eigenvalue threshold (1/k = 0.500), Dimensions 1-2 are recommended for retention, collectively explaining 63.0% of the total variance. Dimension 1 is the strongest, explaining 34.1% of variance, with Dimension 2 explaining an additional 28.9%.
Notes The relatively balanced explanation of variance across Dimensions 1 and 2 suggests that the two geographic variables, Dem_Desc and edr, are capturing distinct but complementary axes of geographic variation in the dataset.


hs.char.mca <- FactoMineR::MCA(hs.char, graph = FALSE)

eig.val <- get_eigenvalue(hs.char.mca)

eig.val

      eigenvalue variance.percent cumulative.variance.percent
Dim.1  0.6825636         34.12818                    34.12818
Dim.2  0.5800517         29.00258                    63.13076
Dim.3  0.4199483         20.99742                    84.12818
Dim.4  0.3174364         15.87182                   100.00000





COS2 - High School Characteristics
Overview COS2 measures the quality of representation of each variable category across the two retained dimensions. All categories show adequate representation with total COS2 values ranging from 0.588 to 0.681.
Dimension 1 - Urban/Southwest Central Axis Urban/town/rural mix (0.671) and EDR 6E Southwest Central (0.629) load most strongly on Dimension 1, suggesting this axis captures variation associated with more urban and centrally located communities.
Dimension 2 - Rural/Southwest Axis Entirely rural (0.527) and EDR 8 Southwest (0.461) load most strongly on Dimension 2, suggesting this axis captures variation associated with more rural and southwestern communities.
Notes The clean separation of categories across the two dimensions, with Dem_Desc and edr categories each contributing to both dimensions, confirms that these two geographic variables are capturing distinct but complementary axes of geographic variation.

table <- hs.char.mca$var$cos2 %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`) %>%
  mutate(total = `Dim 1` + `Dim 2`)
  
datatable(table) %>%
  formatCurrency(1:3, digits = 3, "")




fviz_cos2(hs.char.mca, choice = "var", axes = 1:2)











Contributions - High School Characteristics
Overview Contributions measure how much each variable category drives each retained dimension. Categories with higher contributions are the primary drivers of that dimension’s axis of variation.
Dimension 1 - Urban/Southwest Central Axis Dimension 1 is primarily driven by Urban/town/rural mix (41.7%) and EDR 6E Southwest Central (29.5%), with a secondary contribution from EDR 6W Upper Minnesota Valley (14.9%). This axis clearly captures variation associated with more urban communities concentrated in the Southwest Central region.
Dimension 2 - Rural/Southwest Axis Dimension 2 is primarily driven by Entirely rural (39.1%), EDR 6W Upper Minnesota Valley (25.7%), and EDR 8 Southwest (21.9%), with a minor contribution from Town/rural mix (10.2%). This axis captures variation associated with more rural communities concentrated in the western and southwestern portions of the region.
Notes The clean separation of contributions across the two dimensions confirms that Dim_Desc and edr are capturing distinct but complementary geographic axes of variation, with Dimension 1 reflecting an urban/central gradient and Dimension 2 reflecting a rural/peripheral gradient.


table <- hs.char.mca$var$contrib %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`) %>%
  mutate(`Dim 1` = `Dim 1` / 100,
         `Dim 2` = `Dim 2` / 100)

datatable(table) %>%
  formatPercentage(1:2, digits = 1)








Summary - High School Characteristics
Multiple Correspondence Analysis on the high school characteristics variables produced two retained dimensions explaining 63.0% of the total variance. Dimension 1 captured an urban/Southwest Central axis, primarily driven by Urban/town/rural mix and EDR 6E Southwest Central, while Dimension 2 captured a rural/peripheral axis driven by Entirely rural, EDR 6W Upper Minnesota Valley, and EDR 8 Southwest. All category COS2 values were adequate, ranging from 0.588 to 0.681, indicating that both variables are well represented across the two retained dimensions.
The MCA revealed that Dem_Desc and edr are capturing distinct but complementary axes of geographic variation rather than redundant information. As a result, no high school characteristics variables were eliminated following MCA.
Variables to drop;

None

There are 40,543 individuals in the dataset.


Eigenvalues - High School Accomplishments
Overview The MCA on high school accomplishment variables produced 5 dimensions, collectively explaining 100% of the variance in the data. Analysis is restricted to individuals with known PSEO participation status, excluding the 2008-2010 graduation cohorts where PSEO data was unavailable.
Dimension Retention Using the average eigenvalue threshold (1/k = 0.250), Dimensions 1-2 are recommended for retention, collectively explaining 52.1% of the total variance. Dimension 1 is the strongest, explaining 31.8% of variance, with Dimension 2 explaining an additional 20.3%.
Notes The relatively even spread of variance across Dimensions 3-5 suggests that additional axes of variation exist beyond the two retained dimensions, though none meet the threshold for retention.

hs.accomplishments.mca <- FactoMineR::MCA(hs.accomplishments, graph = FALSE)

eig.val <- get_eigenvalue(hs.accomplishments.mca)

eig.val

      eigenvalue variance.percent cumulative.variance.percent
Dim.1  0.3973477         31.78782                    31.78782
Dim.2  0.2531020         20.24816                    52.03598
Dim.3  0.2477070         19.81656                    71.85254
Dim.4  0.2027354         16.21883                    88.07138
Dim.5  0.1491078         11.92862                   100.00000





COS2 - High School Accomplishments
Overview COS2 measures the quality of representation of each variable category across the two retained dimensions. Values range from 0.040 to 0.805, suggesting considerable variation in how well categories are represented in the retained dimensional space.
Well Represented Categories No CTE is the best represented category (0.805), loading almost entirely on Dimension 2. took.ACT (0.649) and pseo.participant (0.592) are also well represented across the two dimensions.
Moderately Represented Categories CTE Concentrator or Completor (0.538) and ap.exam (0.427) show moderate representation, suggesting some variation in these categories extends beyond the two retained dimensions.
Poorly Represented Categories CTE Participant has a notably low total COS2 of 0.040, indicating this category is poorly captured by the two retained dimensions and may be operating independently of the other high school accomplishment variables.
Notes The poor representation of CTE Participant warrants attention and may suggest that this intermediate category carries unique variation not shared with either No CTE or CTE Concentrator or Completor categories.

table <- hs.accomplishments.mca$var$cos2 %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`) %>%
  mutate(total = `Dim 1` + `Dim 2`)
  
datatable(table) %>%
  formatCurrency(1:3, "", digits = 3)




fviz_cos2(hs.accomplishments.mca, choice = "var", axes = 1:2)











Contributions - High School Accomplishments
Overview Contributions measure how much each variable category drives each retained dimension. Categories with higher contributions are the primary drivers of that dimension’s axis of variation.
Dimension 1 - Academic Achievement / College Preparedness Axis Dimension 1 is primarily driven by took.ACT No (23.8%), pseo.participant_1 (21.1%), and ap.exam_1 (20.0%), with a secondary contribution from took.ACT Yes (11.6%). This axis clearly captures variation associated with academic achievement and college preparedness behaviors in high school, distinguishing students who took the ACT, participated in PSEO, and took AP exams from those who did not.
Dimension 2 - CTE Participation Axis Dimension 2 is overwhelmingly driven by No CTE (61.7%) and CTE Concentrator or Completor (22.3%), indicating this axis captures variation associated with CTE participation and achievement. The near-absence of CTE Participant (0.1%) on this dimension suggests that the intermediate CTE category is not meaningfully contributing to this axis of variation.
Notes The very low contributions of CTE Participant across both dimensions, combined with its poor COS2 representation (0.040), raises the question of whether this intermediate category is adding meaningful information to the analysis. This warrants consideration when interpreting subsequent modeling results involving cte.achievement.


table <- hs.accomplishments.mca$var$contrib %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`) %>%
  mutate(`Dim 1` = `Dim 1` / 100,
         `Dim 2` = `Dim 2` / 100)

datatable(table) %>%
  formatPercentage(1:2, digits = 1)








Summary - High School Accomplishments
Multiple Correspondence Analysis on the high school accomplishment variables produced two retained dimensions explaining 52.1% of the total variance, restricted to individuals with known PSEO participation status. Dimension 1 captured a clear academic achievement and college preparedness axis, primarily driven by took.ACT, pseo.participant, and ap.exam, while Dimension 2 captured a CTE participation axis driven almost entirely by No CTE and CTE Concentrator or Completor.
The CTE Participant category showed consistently poor representation across both dimensions, with a total COS2 of 0.040 and minimal contributions to either dimension, suggesting this intermediate category carries unique variation not well captured by the retained dimensional structure. Despite this, cte.achievement was retained in its original form as the poor representation of CTE Participant may reflect genuine independence rather than redundancy, and collapsing or dropping the category would require further substantive justification. No high school accomplishment variables were eliminated following MCA.
Variables to drop

None

There are 50,830 individuals in the dataset.


MCA analysis

Eigenvalues - Postsecondary Variables
Overview The MCA on postsecondary variables produced 14 meaningful dimensions, collectively explaining 100% of the variance in the data. Dimensions 15-20 have effectively zero eigenvalues, reflecting expected linear dependencies among structurally related postsecondary variables.
Dimension Retention Based on a combination of the average eigenvalue threshold (1/k = 0.200) and a clear elbow in the variance explained after Dimension 2, Dimensions 1-3 are recommended for retention, collectively explaining 52.9% of the total variance. Dimensions 1 and 2 are clearly dominant, explaining 22.5% and 20.0% respectively, with a notable drop to Dimension 3 at 10.4%.
Notes The structural relationships among postsecondary variables, such as ps.grad, ps.grad.location, and highest.cred.level, likely account for the linear dependencies observed in the near-zero eigenvalue dimensions. The relatively complex eigenvalue structure reflects the richness of variation captured by this variable group.


ps.mca <- FactoMineR::MCA(ps, graph = FALSE)

eig.val <- get_eigenvalue(ps.mca)

eig.val

         eigenvalue variance.percent cumulative.variance.percent

COS2 - Postsecondary Variables
Overview COS2 measures the quality of representation of each variable category across the three retained dimensions. Values range considerably from 0.010 to 1.000, revealing both strong redundancy among some variables and poor representation of others.
Perfectly or Near-Perfectly Represented Categories Several categories achieve near-perfect total COS2 values, including ps.grad Attending ps (0.998), ps.grad.InstitutionSector Attending ps (0.998), highest.cred.level Attending ps (0.998), and ps.grad.location Attending ps (0.998). Similarly, ps.grad No (0.970), highest.cred.level Did not graduate ps (0.970), and ps.grad.location Did not graduate ps (0.972) are nearly identical in their representation. These patterns strongly suggest that these categories are capturing the same underlying variation across multiple variables, indicating considerable redundancy among ps.grad, ps.grad.location, and highest.cred.level.
Well Represented Categories ps.grad.InstitutionSector_4 (Public 2-year, 0.909), highest.cred.level Bachelor degree (0.767), and ps.grad.InstitutionSector Never attended ps (0.587) are well represented across the three retained dimensions.
Moderately Represented Categories attended.ps (0.587), ps.grad.InstitutionSector_1 (Public 4-year, 0.562), highest.cred.level Associate degree (0.451), and ps.grad.location PS grad outside MN (0.289) show moderate representation, suggesting some variation in these categories extends beyond the three retained dimensions.
Poorly Represented Categories Several categories show notably poor representation, including ps.grad.InstitutionSector_11 (Private other, 0.010), ps.grad.InstitutionSector_10 (Foreign institutions, 0.078), highest.cred.level Master degree or higher (0.099), and ps.grad.location PS grad region categories (0.170-0.188). These categories carry unique variation not well captured by the three retained dimensions.
Notes The near-identical COS2 patterns across ps.grad, ps.grad.location, and highest.cred.level for the Attending ps and Did not graduate ps categories strongly suggest redundancy among these variables that warrants careful consideration in variable retention decisions.


table <- ps.mca$var$cos2 %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`, `Dim 3`) %>%
  mutate(total = `Dim 1` + `Dim 2` + `Dim 3`)
  
datatable(table) %>%
  formatCurrency(1:4, "", digits = 3)




fviz_cos2(ps.mca, choice = "var", axes = 1:3)











MCA Contributions - Postsecondary Variables
Overview Contributions measure how much each variable category drives each retained dimension. Categories with higher contributions are the primary drivers of that dimension’s axis of variation. The contribution results reveal striking redundancy among several postsecondary variables, with near-identical contributions appearing across multiple variables for the same underlying construct.
Dimension 1 - Did Not Graduate Postsecondary Axis Dimension 1 is driven by near-identical contributions from attended.ps No (9.5%), ps.grad No (11.5%), highest.cred.level Did not graduate ps (11.5%), and ps.grad.location Did not graduate ps (11.5%). The near-identical contributions of these categories across four separate variables strongly indicates that these variables are capturing the same underlying construct — whether an individual did not attend or graduate from postsecondary education.
Dimension 2 - Currently Attending Postsecondary Axis Dimension 2 is almost entirely driven by four identical contributions of 21.8% each from ps.grad Attending ps, ps.grad.InstitutionSector Attending ps, highest.cred.level Attending ps, and ps.grad.location Attending ps. This perfect replication of contributions across four variables is unambiguous evidence of redundancy, as all four variables are capturing exactly the same information about individuals currently attending postsecondary education.
Dimension 3 - Institution Type and Credential Level Axis Dimension 3 is driven by ps.grad.InstitutionSector_4 (Public 2-year, 27.3%), highest.cred.level Bachelor degree (15.2%), highest.cred.level Less than Associate Degree (12.1%), and highest.cred.level Associate degree (13.7%). This dimension captures meaningful variation in the type of institution attended and the level of credential attained, representing the most substantively informative axis of variation among the postsecondary variables.
Notes The contribution results provide compelling evidence of redundancy among ps.grad, ps.grad.location, highest.cred.level, and attended.ps, particularly for the Attending ps and Did not graduate ps categories which contribute identically across multiple variables. This redundancy warrants serious consideration of variable elimination prior to modeling, with Dimension 3 suggesting that institution type and credential level may be the most informative non-redundant postsecondary variables.


table <- ps.mca$var$contrib %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  select(`Dim 1`, `Dim 2`, `Dim 3`) %>%
  mutate(`Dim 1` = `Dim 1` / 100,
         `Dim 2` = `Dim 2` /100,
         `Dim 3` = `Dim 3` / 100)

datatable(table) %>%
  formatPercentage(1:3, digits = 1)








MCA Summary - Postsecondary Variables
Multiple Correspondence Analysis on the postsecondary variables produced three retained dimensions explaining 52.9% of the total variance. Dimension 1 captured a did not graduate postsecondary axis, driven by near-identical contributions from attended.ps, ps.grad, highest.cred.level, and ps.grad.location, all reflecting whether an individual did not attend or complete postsecondary education. Dimension 2 captured a currently attending postsecondary axis, again driven by perfectly identical contributions across ps.grad, ps.grad.InstitutionSector, highest.cred.level, and ps.grad.location, providing unambiguous evidence of redundancy among these variables for this particular category. Dimension 3 captured a more substantively distinct axis of institution type and credential level, primarily driven by public 2-year institution attendance and varying credential levels.
While the MCA revealed considerable redundancy among the postsecondary variables, all five variables were retained on substantive grounds. Each variable contributes meaningful and distinct information to the modeling context, particularly given the importance of credential level distinctions, geographic location of attendance, and institution type in understanding regional workforce participation outcomes.

title: CTEDemographics
text:
Data prep
Now I’m going to prep the CTE demographics dataset to join with the master. I will first load the entire CTE demographics dataset, clean it up, and then join with my master/SW graduate dataset.


cte.demographics.original <- read_csv('/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/CRPD 2 (angelique.workman@state.mn.us)/CTEDemographic.csv')  %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID))

x




PersonID


FiscalYearTimeID


Grade


BasicParticipant


BasicConcentrator


BasicCompletor


Concentrator150






The original dataset provides whether the student was a basic participant, basic concentrator, basic completor, and/or a centrentrator 150 when taking a CTE course. It looks like each observation is unique by grade of the student. This means that one PersonID can have multiple observations.
In order to determine how I want to organize this data so that each observation represents the full characteristics of each PersonID I need to understand how these values are defined.

Basic Participant: Any grade 9-12 student who successfully completes one or more state-approved CTE course(s).
Basic Concentrator: this is the old standard for years and required the student complete 240 hours. Any student successfully enrolled in a single CTE field for more than 240 hours (successful is defined as passing grade).
Basic Completor: any concentrator who attains academic and technical knowledge and skills and graduates from high school.
Concentrator 150: This is the new standard - Any grade 9-12 student who successfully completes 150+ course hours (or more) as part of a state-approved secondary CTE program, within one career field.

Judging by these definitions, it seems that we will want three categories;

Those that participated in a CTE course but were not a concentrator or a completor, and
Those that participated in a CTE course and graduated as a concentrator.
Those that did not participate in a CTE course.

So essentially, filtering this dataset to provide the “highest level achieved” in terms of CTE coursework. We will focus on filtering this dataset to the highest level achieved and will then join with the graduate list which will have all the individuals that did not participate in any CTE courses.


graduate <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-4.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID)

cte.concentrator.cat <- cte.demographics.original %>%
  gather(key = "cte.achievement", value = "yes.or.no", 4:7) %>%
  filter(yes.or.no != "Null") %>%
  filter(yes.or.no != "NULL") %>%
  filter(yes.or.no != "N") %>%
  mutate(cte.achievement = as.factor(cte.achievement),
         yes.or.no = as.factor(yes.or.no),
         cte.achievement.code = ifelse(cte.achievement == "BasicParticipant", 0, 1)) %>%
  select(PersonID, cte.achievement, cte.achievement.code) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(cte.achievement.code = sum(cte.achievement.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(cte.achievement = ifelse(cte.achievement.code == 0, "CTE Participant", "CTE Concentrator or Completor")) %>%
  select(PersonID, cte.achievement) %>%
  right_join(graduate, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(cte.achievement = ifelse(is.na(cte.achievement), "No CTE", cte.achievement))

When joined with the master file, there were 43,439 that were either a CTE participant or concentrator.
Next, let’s join it all up.


master.5 <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-4.csv") %>%
  left_join(cte.concentrator.cat, by = "PersonID")

ctedemographics.master <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-4.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID) %>%
  left_join(cte.concentrator.cat, by = "PersonID")

write_csv(ctedemographics.master, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/ctedemographics_master.csv")

After joining with the master dataset, we have 54,063 rows and 48 columns.



Summary of CTE demographics
We will summarize the CTE demographics data by rural-ness and region to see if there’s any significant differences.
First, lets see what percentage of the graduates by cte demographics categories.


cte.dem.summary.total <- master.5 %>%
  mutate(cte.achievement = as.factor(cte.achievement)) %>%
  tabyl(cte.achievement)

datatable(cte.dem.summary.total, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top", rownames = FALSE) %>%
  formatPercentage(3) %>%
  formatCurrency(2, "", digits = 0)






The cross-tabs show a significant difference between the percentage of students by categorization and RUCA categories.


RUCA TableRUCA Cross-tabs



cte.dem.summary.ruca.table <- master.5 %>%
  mutate(cte.achievement = as.factor(cte.achievement)) %>%
  tabyl(Dem_Desc, cte.achievement) %>%
  adorn_percentages("row")

datatable(cte.dem.summary.ruca.table, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top", rownames = FALSE) %>%
  formatPercentage(2:4)

Lets take a look at the county map.


County MapCounty Table



cte.dem.summary.county.map <- master.5 %>%
  mutate(cte.achievement = as.factor(cte.achievement)) %>%
  tabyl(county.name, cte.achievement) %>%
  adorn_percentages("row") %>%
  gather(key = "cte.category", value = "pct", 2:4) %>%
  left_join(counties.regions[,c(1,2)], by = c("county.name" = "Name")) %>%
  left_join(mn_counties[,c(5,7)], by = "countyfp")

cte.dem.summary.county.map.plot<- ggplot(cte.dem.summary.county.map) +
  facet_wrap(~cte.category, ncol = 2) +
  geom_sf_interactive(color = "grey85", aes(geometry = geometry, fill = pct, data_id = countyfp, tooltip = paste(county.name, "\nCTE achievement category: ", cte.category, "\nPercent of graduates: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  theme_sf+
  scale_fill_fermenter(palette = "PuBu", direction = 1, labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Percent of graduates in each county location of high school by\nCTE achievement\n") +
  theme(legend.box.margin = margin(50, 0, 0, -100),
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        plot.title.position = "plot")

girafe(ggobj = cte.dem.summary.county.map.plot, height_svg = 10, width_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

title: CTEEnrollment
text:
The next dataset is individuals that were enrolled in CTE courses. I’m first going to load the entire dataset, then clean it up, and then I will merge it with my master/NW graduate dataset.


cte.enrollment.original <- read_csv('/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/CRPD 2 (angelique.workman@state.mn.us)/CTEEnrollment.csv') %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID)) %>%
  filter(FiscalYearTimeID != "NULL") %>%
  mutate(CareerCluster = ifelse(CareerCluster == "26" & CareerClusterName == "Diversified Youth Apprenticeship", "25", CareerCluster),
         CareerCluster = ifelse(CareerCluster == "NULL", "0", CareerCluster),
         CareerCluster = str_replace(CareerCluster, "Design", "11"),
         CareerCluster = as.integer(CareerCluster))


career.cluster.codes.names.original <- read_xlsx('/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/SLEDS/CTEEnrollment/career-cluster-codes-names_from SLEDS_March2025.xlsx') %>%
  mutate(cte.year = ifelse(Introduced > 2020, 2021, Discontinued),
         cte.year = ifelse(is.na(Introduced) & is.na(Discontinued), 2021, cte.year),
         cte.year = ifelse(is.na(cte.year), 2020, cte.year),
         CareerClusterName.new = as_factor(CareerClusterName.new))


career.cluster.codes.names.addendum <- data.frame(`CareerCluster` = c(0, 1, 2),
                                                  CareerClusterName.new = c("NULL", "Agriculture, Food, and Natural Resources", "Architecture & Construction"),
                                                  Status = c("Retired", "Retired", "Retired"),
                                                  Introduced = c(2021, 2021, 2021),
                                                  Discontinued = c(2021, 2021, 2021),
                                                  cte.year = c(2020, 2020, 2020))

career.cluster.codes.names <- career.cluster.codes.names.original %>%
  rbind(career.cluster.codes.names.addendum)

cte.enrollment.master <- cte.enrollment.original %>%
  mutate(year = str_sub(FiscalYearTimeID, 1, 4),
         year = as.integer(year),
         cte.year = ifelse(year < 2021, 2020, 2021)) %>%
  left_join(career.cluster.codes.names[,c(1,2,6)], by = c("CareerCluster", "cte.year")) %>%
  select(-CareerClusterName, -year, -cte.year) %>%
  rename(CareerClusterName = CareerClusterName.new)

kable(head(cte.enrollment.master))

CareerClusterName






This dataset provides each CTE course taken by a student. This means that there were 2,109,868 instances of a student taking a CTE course. There are 12 columns providing the PersonID taking the course, the fiscal year, the K12 organization, along with details about the actual course itself.
There are two goals with this dataset.

Format it so each observation represents a single PersonID along with the total number of CTE courses they took.
Format it so each observation represents a single PersonID along with the number of CTE courses taken in each CareerCluster.

I’m using careerclusters due to their specificity.


graduate <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-3.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID)

cte.n.total.courses <- cte.enrollment.master %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(total.cte.courses.taken = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  right_join(graduate, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(total.cte.courses.taken = ifelse(is.na(total.cte.courses.taken), 0, total.cte.courses.taken))

A total of 49,188 individuals took at least one CTE course in the dataset.
Next I’m going to create a dataset that calculates the number of CTE courses each individual has taken by CareerCluster.


cte.n.careercluster <- cte.enrollment.master %>%
  group_by(PersonID, CareerClusterName) %>%
  summarise(cte.n.careercluster = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  right_join(graduate, by = "PersonID") %>%
  complete(PersonID, CareerClusterName, fill = list(cte.n.careercluster = 0)) %>%
  drop_na(CareerClusterName) %>%
  mutate(CareerClusterName,
         CareerClusterName = str_replace(CareerClusterName, "Arts, A/V Technology, & Communication", "Arts, A/V Technology & Communication")) %>%
  spread(key = CareerClusterName, value = cte.n.careercluster) %>%
  select(PersonID, 2, 4:18, 20:ncol(.))

There seems to be a minor coding error in the careercluster column. For one particular class, it was consistently labeled “Information Technology” even though the field requires a number value. In addition, “information Technology” doesn’t seem to match the course due to the following information;
Program Course Name: Family and Consumer Science Course title: Apparrel CareerCluster: Design CareerClusterName: 6 (which should be a letter value, not a code).
Due to this information, I recoded anything that was “Information Technology” for that particular course as a “6”.
In addition, there were some errors in careercluster 26, which is supposed to be “Administrative only” but had a number of “Youth Apprenticeships” in it that I recoded to CareerCluster 25.
After doing this, the dataset has all individuals/graduates with a column for each CTE course CareerCluster containing the number of courses they took within that CareerCluster. I will summarise below to see how many students took courses within each CareerCluster.
Now lets join the CTE enrollment data with the master dataset.


master.3 <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-3.csv")

  left_join(cte.n.total.courses, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(cte.n.careercluster, by = "PersonID")

cteenrollment.master <- master.3 %>%
  select(PersonID) %>%
  left_join(cte.n.total.courses, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(cte.n.careercluster, by = "PersonID")

write_csv(cteenrollment.master, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/cteenrollment_master.csv")

title: CTEEnrollment
section: Summary - Number of CTE courses taken by a student
text:
Summary - Number of CTE courses taken by a student

We’ll start with summarising the number of cte courses the observations have taken.


cte.n.courses.summary.total <- master.4 %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(total.cte.courses.taken),
            median = median(total.cte.courses.taken),
            sd = sd(total.cte.courses.taken),
            n = comma(n(), accuracy = 1),
            min = min(total.cte.courses.taken),
            max = max(total.cte.courses.taken)) %>%
  mutate(population = "All graduates",
         mean = comma(mean, accuracy = .01),
         sd = comma(sd, accuracy = .01))

cte.n.courses.summary.ctestudents <- master.4 %>%
  filter(total.cte.courses.taken > 0) %>%
  summarise(mean = mean(total.cte.courses.taken),
            median = median(total.cte.courses.taken),
            sd = sd(total.cte.courses.taken),
            n = comma(n(), accuracy = 1),
            min = min(total.cte.courses.taken),
            max = max(total.cte.courses.taken)) %>%
  mutate(population = "Only graduates with CTE courses > 0",
         mean = comma(mean, accuracy = .01),
         sd = comma(sd, accuracy = .01))
  
cte.n.courses.summary.total.ctestudents <- cte.n.courses.summary.total %>%
  rbind(cte.n.courses.summary.ctestudents) %>%
  select(population, n, mean, median, sd, min, max)

datatable(cte.n.courses.summary.total.ctestudents, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top")






Below is a chart showing the distribution of CTE courses taken by individuals. This shows that a a large majority of students take 5 courses or less.


master.4.hist <- master.4 %>%
  mutate(key = seq(n())) %>%
  select(key, total.cte.courses.taken)

cte.n.courses.taken.dist.plot <- ggplot(master.4.hist, aes(total.cte.courses.taken)) +
  geom_histogram_interactive(aes(data_id = key, tooltip = paste("N = ", comma(..count.., accuracy = 1), "\nPercent: ", percent(..count.. / sum(..count..), accuracy = .1)))) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Histogram - N CTE courses")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma)+
  theme_line+
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16))

  girafe(ggobj = cte.n.courses.taken.dist.plot, width_svg = 10) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))






The following table shows that nearly three-quarters of the students took at least one CTE course.


cte.pct <- master.4 %>%
  mutate(took.CTE = ifelse(total.cte.courses.taken > 0, "Yes", "No")) %>%
  group_by(took.CTE) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup()  %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n))

datatable(cte.pct, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top", rownames = FALSE,
          options = list(columnDefs = list(list(className = "dt-center", targets = 1)))) %>%
  formatCurrency(2, "", digits = 0) %>%
  formatPercentage(3, digits = 1)






Up next is to see if the percentage of students taking a CTE course varies significantly by RUCA group.


RUCA TableRUCA Cross-tabs



cte.yes.ruca <- master.4 %>%
  mutate(cte.yes = ifelse(total.cte.courses.taken > 0, "Yes", "No")) %>%
  tabyl(Dem_Desc, cte.yes) %>%
  adorn_percentages("row") %>%
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits = 1)

cte.n.ruca <- master.4 %>%
  filter(total.cte.courses.taken > 0 ) %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  summarise(mean = comma(mean(total.cte.courses.taken), accuracy = .1),
            median = comma(median(total.cte.courses.taken), accuracy = 1),
            sd = comma(sd(total.cte.courses.taken), accuracy = .1),
            n = comma(n(), accuracy = 1),
            min = comma(min(total.cte.courses.taken), accuracy = 1),
            max = comma(max(total.cte.courses.taken), accuracy = 1)) %>%
  ungroup()

datatable(cte.yes.ruca, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top")




tags$br()

cte.yes.no.ruca <- master.4 %>%
  mutate(cte.yes = ifelse(total.cte.courses.taken > 0, "Yes", "No")) 

CrossTable(cte.yes.no.ruca$Dem_Desc, cte.yes.no.ruca$cte.yes, expected = TRUE, prop.t = FALSE, prop.c = FALSE, prop.chisq = FALSE)

title: CTEEnrollment
section: Summary - CareerCluster of CTE courses taken
text:
Summary - CareerCluster of CTE courses taken
Let’s dig into the breadth of CTE course concentrations a graduate would take by looking at the proportion of graduates that took at least one course within a concentration.


cte.cf.prop <- master.4 %>%
  gather(key = "CareerCluster", value = "n.courses", `ACTE-SPED`:`Youth Apprenticeship`) %>%
  mutate(cte.cf.taken = ifelse(n.courses > 0, "Yes", "No")) %>%
  tabyl(CareerCluster, cte.cf.taken) %>%
  mutate(pct.taken.cf = Yes / (Yes + No)) %>% 
  arrange(desc(pct.taken.cf))

datatable(cte.cf.prop, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top", rownames = FALSE,
          options = list(scrollX = TRUE)) %>%
  formatCurrency(2:3, "", digits = 0) %>%
  formatPercentage(4)






Now we will take a look at the breakdown of the number of times a CTE courses has been taken within a CareerCluster for each PersonID. I expect there to be a lot of variation in this data since different schools might focus on different types of CTE offerings depending on the local employment makeup.


cte.cf.concentration.total <- master.4 %>%
  select(PersonID,  `ACTE-SPED`:`Youth Apprenticeship`) %>%
  gather(key = "CareerCluster", value = "n.courses", 2:ncol(.)) %>%
  group_by(CareerCluster) %>%
  summarise(n = sum(n.courses)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pct = percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = .1)) %>%
  arrange(CareerCluster)

  datatable(cte.cf.concentration.total, class = "cell-border stripe", filter = "top", rownames = FALSE)






Lets see how this looks broken down by county.


cte.cf.concentration.county <- master.4 %>%
  select(PersonID, county.name, countyfp,  `ACTE-SPED`:`Youth Apprenticeship`) %>%
  gather(key = "CareerCluster", value = "n.courses",  `ACTE-SPED`:`Youth Apprenticeship`) %>%
  group_by(county.name, countyfp, CareerCluster) %>%
  summarise(n = sum(n.courses)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(county.name, countyfp) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  rename(CareerClusterName = CareerCluster) %>%
  left_join(mn_counties[,c(5,7)], by = "countyfp")

cte.cf.concentration.county.map <- ggplot(data = filter(cte.cf.concentration.county, pct > .05)) +
  facet_wrap(~CareerClusterName, ncol = 2,
             labeller = label_wrap_gen(15)) +
  geom_sf_interactive(color = "grey85", aes(geometry = geometry, fill = pct, data_id = countyfp, tooltip = paste(county.name, "\nCareerCluster: ", CareerClusterName, "\nPercent of courses taken in this CareerCluster: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  theme_sf+
  scale_fill_fermenter(palette = "PuBu", direction = 1, labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = str_wrap("Percent of CTE courses taken within each CareerCluster by county", 39)) +
  theme(legend.box.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0),
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        strip.text = element_text(size = 8))

girafe(ggobj = cte.cf.concentration.county.map, height_svg = 20) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))






The next big question is whether these percentages vary by year. Are there any trends?


cte.cf.concentration.year <- master.4 %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, `ACTE-SPED`:`Youth Apprenticeship`) %>%
  gather(key = "CareerCluster", value = "n.courses", `ACTE-SPED`:`Youth Apprenticeship`) %>%
  group_by(grad.year, CareerCluster) %>%
  summarise(n = sum(n.courses)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(grad.year) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(grad.year > 2006) %>%
  mutate(data_id = seq(n())) %>%
  rename(CareerClusterName = CareerCluster) 


cte.cf.concentration.year.plot <- ggplot(cte.cf.concentration.year, aes(grad.year, pct, color = CareerClusterName)) +
  geom_point_interactive(size = 3, aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(CareerClusterName, "\nYear: ", grad.year, "\nCareerCluster: ", CareerClusterName, "\nPercent of CTE courses taken: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Percent of taken CTE courses")+
  geom_label(data = filter(cte.cf.concentration.year, grad.year == max(cte.cf.concentration.year$grad.year)), aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, size = 5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1900, 2050, 2),
                     guide = guide_axis(n.dodge = 2)) +
  theme_line+
  scale_color_discrete(guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 14))

  girafe(ggobj = cte.cf.concentration.year.plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 7) %>%
    girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                   opts_toolbar(saveaspng = FALSE),
                   opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))







write_csv(master.4, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-4.csv")

title: Assessments
text:
Okay, lets check out assessments. This is the entire assessment dataset, which I will first clean up and then join it with our master/SW graduate dataset.


assessments.original <- read_csv('/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/CRPD 2 (angelique.workman@state.mn.us)/K12Assessment.csv')  %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID),
         TestName = as.factor(TestName),
         Subject = as.factor(Subject),
         AchievementLevel = as.factor(AchievementLevel)) 

kable(head(assessments.original))

x




PersonID


TestName


Subject


Grade


AchievementLevel






We have 6,198,938 rows and 5 columns in this dataset. Here are the descriptions for each of the columns.

K12AsstTimeID: Day, month, and year the assessment took place.
TestName: Name of the test taken. There are 12 different tests in the dataset -

ACCESS: English proficiency,
ALTACCESS: English proficiency for special education or meet requirements
MCA-II & MCA-III: Minnesota Comprehsensive Assessments (series 2 and 3)
MOD-II, MOD-III: Modified Comprehensive Assessment, used only until 2014
MTAS & MTAS-III: Minnesota Test of Academic Skills
MTELL: an alternate assessment for students learning English (like the MTAS or MOD). MTELL was only administered from 2007-2010 during the series-II of MCAs.
SOLOM: Minnesota Student Oral Language Observation Matrix
TEAE: one of two assessments that Minnesota used to meet the requirements to test ELs in all four domains of language (the other was SOLOM). TEAE was used 2002 until we changed to ACCESS in 2012. SOLOM was used 2004 until we changed to ACCESS in 2012.

Subject: Classifies each test that is given to a student based on the areas measured. There are 8 factors;

A: Aural (listening) - ACCESS & ALTACCESS only
C: Composite - ACCESS & ALTACCESS only
L: Listening/Speaking
M: Math
O: Oral - ACCESS & ALTACCESS only
R: Reading
S: Science
W: Writing

GradeTested: Grade student was enrolled in when performing assessment.
AchievementLevel: Used to count the number of students in each achievement level by group such as race, gender, and ethnicity. It must be calculated from other students at a similar graden and subject for a specific year.

ACCESS students are graded on a language proficiency scale of 1 through 6.
ALT-ACCESS students are graded using a P1 through P3 and A1 through A3 scale.
There are 4 different levels for the MCA test

D: Does not meet standards
E: Exceeds standards
M: Meets Standards
P: Partially meets standards

SOLOM tests are 1-5 scale with 5 being the highest
TEAE tests are 1-5 scale with 5 being the highest.


There are two ways in which we are going to code our graduates.
The first is to identify whether a student has been identified as an English learner. This means filtering students that have taken the ACCESS, ALT-ACCESS, MTELL, MTAS, TEAE, MOD-II, MOD-III, or SOLOM at least once in their career.
The second is be determining their highest achievement level in each subject of the MCA tests within the dataset. The dataset provides each instance of a test for each student so many are students are listed numerous times - once for each time they took a test.
Lets do this in two separate files and then join each with the master file.
Starting with identifying the EL students.


el <- assessments.original %>%
  filter(!TestName %in% c("MCA-II", "MCA-III")) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  select(PersonID) %>%
  mutate(english.learner = 1)

kable(head(el))




PersonID
english.learner

kable(names(el))




x




PersonID


english.learner






This dataset identified 37,906 individuals who were identified as an english learner at least one time in the original dataset.
Now we will determine the highest achievement level for each subject for each student.


mca <- assessments.original %>%
  filter(TestName %in% c("MCA-II", "MCA-III")) %>%
  filter(AchievementLevel != "NULL") %>%
  droplevels() %>%
  mutate(AchievementLevelCode = ifelse(AchievementLevel == "D", 1,
                                       ifelse(AchievementLevel == "P", 2,
                                              ifelse(AchievementLevel == "M", 3, 4))))

mca.highest <- mca %>%
  group_by(PersonID, Subject) %>%
  filter(AchievementLevelCode == max(AchievementLevelCode)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(PersonID, Subject, AchievementLevelCode) %>%
  mutate(mca.subject = paste("MCA.", Subject, sep = "")) %>%
  select(PersonID, mca.subject, AchievementLevelCode) %>%
  mutate(id = seq(n())) %>%
  spread(key = mca.subject, value = AchievementLevelCode) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  fill(MCA.M, .direction = c("downup")) %>%
  fill(MCA.R, .direction = "downup") %>%
  fill(MCA.S, .direction = "downup") %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  select(-id)

This dataset has 525,434 rows and 4 columns. In this dataset, I have recoded the AchievementLevel so that 1 = Do not meet standards, 2 = Partially meets standards, 3 = Meets standards, and 4 = Exceeds standards. I will summarize this column in the summary section below.
Now it’s time to join the datasets.


master.7 <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-5.csv") %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID)) %>%
  left_join(el, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(mca.highest, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(english.learner = ifelse(is.na(english.learner), 0, english.learner))

assessments.master <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-5.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID) %>%
  mutate(PersonID = as.integer(PersonID)) %>%
  left_join(el, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(mca.highest, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(english.learner = ifelse(is.na(english.learner), 0, english.learner))

write_csv(assessments.master, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/assessments_master.csv")

title: Assessments
text:
MCA - Math
We will start with analyzing math and RUCA categories.


RUCA TableRUCA Cross-tabs



mca.highest.summary.ruca <- master.7 %>%
  select(Dem_Desc, MCA.M) %>%
  mutate(MCA.M = ifelse(MCA.M == 1, "D",
                              ifelse(MCA.M == 2, "P",
                                     ifelse(MCA.M == 3, "M",
                                            ifelse(MCA.M == 4, "E", MCA.M)))),
         MCA.M = fct_relevel(MCA.M, "D", "P", "M", "E")) %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc, MCA.M) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(Dem_Desc) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(data_id = as.character(seq(n())))

mca.highest.ruca.plot <- ggplot(mca.highest.summary.ruca, aes(Dem_Desc, pct, fill = MCA.M, group = MCA.M)) +
    geom_col_interactive(position = "dodge", aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste("MCA - Math", "\nGrade: ", MCA.M, "\nNumber of students with this grade: ", comma(n, accuracy = 1), "\nPercent of students: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
    geom_label(position = position_dodge(width = .9), aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, color = "black", size = 5) +
    labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Student grades within RUCA category for MCA - Math")+
    scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
    theme_bar+
    scale_fill_manual(values= brewer.pal(n = 5, "RdYlBu"),
                       guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom",
          text = element_text(size = 16),
          axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = mca.highest.ruca.plot) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

title: Employment
text:
Who has an employment record?
First, we want to figure out who from our master dataset has a MN employment record at each of the time instances being measured - graduation year, and graduation year +2, graduation year +7. Here are the columns I want when finished;

PersonID has no MN employment record (Grad year, grad year +2, grad year +7)

No MN emp record - Has never worked in Minnesota
After 2023 - the grad year +X is after 2023.




grad.years <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/Master-10.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID, hs.grad.year) %>%
  mutate(hs.grad.year.2 = hs.grad.year + 2,
         hs.grad.year.7 = hs.grad.year + 7) %>%
  gather(key = "hs.grad.year", value = "year", 2:4)

mn.emp.record.year <- employment.original %>%
  mutate(CalendarYear = as.integer(str_sub(TimeID, 1,4))) %>%
  select(PersonID, CalendarYear) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, CalendarYear) %>%
  mutate(mn.emp.record = "MN emp record")

mn.emp.record <- grad.years %>%
  left_join(mn.emp.record.year, by = c("PersonID", "year" = "CalendarYear")) %>%
  mutate(mn.emp.record = ifelse(year > 2023, "After 2023", mn.emp.record),
         mn.emp.record = ifelse(is.na(mn.emp.record), "No MN emp record", mn.emp.record),
         label = paste("mn.emp.record.", hs.grad.year, sep = "")) %>%
  select(PersonID, label, mn.emp.record) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = label, values_from = mn.emp.record)

title: Employment
text:
Filter for “meaningful” employment
The biggest issue is how do we get a meaningful indicator on whether they were employed in the region and whether they are employed in an industry that matches their CTE experience that wasn’t just a “summer job”. We need to filter out summer jobs between college years, as well as adapt to different paths that students take.
We will define “meaningful employment” as an employment experience in which an individual works for one particular employer for 1,000 hours worked in any given year.
However, this isn’t going to just be checking to see if an individual has worked at an employer for 1,000 hours at graduation year, grad year +2, grad year +7 and grad year +12. Rather, it’s going to be checking on whether they are working at an employer at grad year, grad year +2, grad year +7 and grad year +12 for which they worked 1,000 hours for an given year.
For example, a person may be employed with an employer for a few years working full-time, but part-way through grad year +7, they decide to move on. I want to make sure we capture that that individual was employed in meaningful employment even though it wasn’t full-time that particular year.
The same can go the other way. Perhaps an individual just starts employment at grad year +7 but does work full-time with that particular employer form multiple years. We will want to count that as well.
In addition, I want to capture if someone is working at an employer but isn’t quite “meaningful” but turns into meaningful later on. We will count that as well.
This will be a couple step process.

First, we will filter out the employment.original data so that only meaningful employment for each PersonID is included along with the years worked at EmploymentOrganizationID.
Collect EmploymentOrganizationID for all the years that each PersonID worked meaningful employment.
Create a new datalist of each PersonID in the master datalist with grad year, grad year +2, and +7.
Then, we will cross reference the grad year list with employment year to see if meaningful employment matches.

We will end with the following columns and labels;

PersonID has had meaningful employment since graduating high school (Grad year, grad year +2, grad year +7)

No MN emp record - Has no MN emp record
No meaningful emp - Has MN emp record but never
Meaningful emp - Has MN emp record and is meaningful
After 2023 - the grad year +X is after 2023.




meaningful.employer <- employment.original %>%
  mutate(CalendarYear = as.integer(str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4))) %>%
  group_by(PersonID, EmploymentOrganizationID, CalendarYear) %>%
  summarize(EmployeeQuarterlyHoursWorked = sum(EmployeeQuarterlyHoursWorked)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  filter(EmployeeQuarterlyHoursWorked > 999) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  distinct(PersonID, EmploymentOrganizationID) %>%
  right_join(mn.emp.record[,c(1)], by = "PersonID")

kable(head(meaningful.employer))

This dataset has 91,552 rows which represents an PersonID and EmploymentOrganizationID with which they worked 1,000 or more hours had been worked in any given year of employment with that organization. Each PersonID is also in our master datalist.
Next, we need to make sure we are not missing any years in which a PersonID had meaningful employment with an OrganizationID but for other years they may not have been “meaningful”.


meaningful.emp <- employment.original %>%
  mutate(CalendarYear = as.integer(str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4))) %>%
  select(PersonID, CalendarYear, EmploymentOrganizationID) %>%
  right_join(meaningful.employer, by = c("PersonID", "EmploymentOrganizationID")) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, EmploymentOrganizationID, CalendarYear)

Now, we will cross-reference these lists to confirm whether the PersonID from the grad.years datalist were working “meaningful employment” (as defined in above section) in each of grad year times.


meaningful.emp.grad.years <- grad.years %>%
  gather(key = "grad.years", value= "year", 2:4) %>%
  left_join(meaningful.emp, by = c("PersonID", "year" = "CalendarYear")) %>%

  filter(!is.na(EmploymentOrganizationID)) %>%
  filter(EmploymentOrganizationID != "After 2023") %>%

         meaningful.emp = "Yes")
