title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
total.cte.courses.taken
Overview One-way ANOVA examined differences in total CTE courses taken across workforce participation outcome groups at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Eta-squared values indicate a small but consistent association across all three time points, with the meaningful regional employment group consistently showing the highest number of CTE courses taken of any group.
Year 1 At one year post-graduation, total CTE courses taken differs significantly across outcome groups (F=388.03, df=4, p<0.001) with a small effect size (η²=0.032). The meaningful regional employment group shows significantly more CTE courses taken than all other groups. The attending postsecondary group shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-1.674, p<0.001), as does the no Minnesota employment record group (estimate=-1.782, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment group (estimate=-0.901, p<0.001). No significant difference is found between meaningful regional and meaningful Minnesota employment groups, suggesting that CTE course-taking does not distinguish between these two stable employment outcomes at year 1.
Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association remains a small effect (F=240.62, df=4, p<0.001, η²=0.027). The meaningful regional employment group continues to show significantly more CTE courses taken than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment group now shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-0.975, p<0.001), a distinction that was not significant at year 1, suggesting that CTE course-taking increasingly differentiates regional from out-of-region employment over time. The no Minnesota employment record group shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-1.316, p<0.001) and not meaningful employment (estimate=-0.513, p<0.001).
Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains a small effect (F=187.13, df=3, p<0.001, η²=0.028). The meaningful regional employment group continues to show the highest number of CTE courses taken, significantly more than all other groups. The meaningful Minnesota employment and no Minnesota employment record groups are indistinguishable from each other (estimate=-0.012, p=0.998), while both show significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimates of -1.302 and -1.314 respectively, p<0.001). The not meaningful employment group shows significantly fewer CTE courses than meaningful regional employment (estimate=-0.783, p<0.001) but significantly more than both meaningful Minnesota employment (estimate=0.519, p<0.001) and no Minnesota employment record (estimate= -0.531, p<0.001).
Summary Total CTE courses taken shows a small but consistent association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (η²=0.032, 0.027, 0.028), notable for its stability rather than decline over time. Despite the modest effect size, the meaningful regional employment group shows the highest number of CTE courses taken at every time point, and the gap between regional and out-of-region employment groups grows over time — from no significant difference between regional and Minnesota employment at year 1 to a difference of approximately 1.3 courses by year 10. Together with the CTE achievement findings, these results provide consistent evidence that CTE participation is associated with stronger long-term regional workforce retention, even if the overall explanatory power of this variable is modest.

One year after graduatingFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating



data <- meaningful.emp.sw %>%
  select(grad.year.1, total.cte.courses.taken) %>%
  filter(grad.year.1 != "After 2023") %>%
  drop_na() %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  mutate(q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
         q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
         iqr = IQR(total.cte.courses.taken),
         low.outlier = q1 - 1.5*iqr,
         high.outlier = q3 + 1.5*iqr) %>%
  filter(total.cte.courses.taken > low.outlier,
         total.cte.courses.taken < high.outlier) %>%
  ungroup()

labels <- data %>%
  group_by(grad.year.1) %>%
  summarize(Min = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .0),
            Q1 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .25),
            Median = median(total.cte.courses.taken),
            Q3 = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, .75),
            Max = quantile(total.cte.courses.taken, 1),
            Mean = mean(total.cte.courses.taken)) %>%
  ungroup()

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year.1, total.cte.courses.taken, fill = grad.year.1, group = grad.year.1)) +
  geom_boxplot_interactive() +
  geom_label(data = labels, aes(x = grad.year.1, y = 5, label = paste("Min: ", comma(Min, accuracy = .1), "\nQ1: ", comma(Q1, accuracy = .1), "\nMedian: ", comma(Median, accuracy = .1), "\nQ3: ", comma(Q3, accuracy = .1), "\nMax: ", comma(Max, accuracy = ), "\nMean: ", comma(Mean, accuracy = .1))), color = "white") +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Boxplots of number of CTE courses by employment state")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::comma,
                     limits = c(0, 6))+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 6, "RdYlBu")) +
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 18),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
section: Summary of Postsecondary Variables and Workforce Participation Outcomes
text:
Summary of Postsecondary Variables and Workforce Participation Outcomes
The following postsecondary variables were examined in relation to workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. All variables are categorical and were examined using cross-tabulation and Cramer’s V. Variables are ranked by Cramer’s V at year 1, with the caveat that several variables show inflated associations at year 1 due to structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category.
Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1

Postsecondary Attendance (Cramer’s V: 0.698, 0.295, 0.108) The strongest association at year 1, largely driven by the near-tautological relationship between postsecondary attendance and the attending postsecondary outcome category. As this structural effect dissipates, the association weakens to weak by year 10. The most substantively important long-term finding is that non-attendees show stronger regional employment attachment while attendees show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment and lower rates of no Minnesota employment record.
Postsecondary Graduation (Cramer’s V: 0.633, 0.330, 0.130) The second strongest association at year 1, also substantially driven by structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category. By year 10 the association weakens to weak, with the most important finding being that postsecondary graduates show considerably higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment while non-graduates show stronger regional attachment and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record.
Highest Credential Level (Cramer’s V: 0.378, 0.246, 0.111) A strong association at year 1 that weakens to weak by year 10. A clear and persistent inverse relationship emerges between credential level and regional workforce attachment — sub-baccalaureate credential holders show the strongest regional employment rates across all time points, while bachelor and master degree holders show increasing tendencies toward meaningful Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. Master degree or higher holders show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record at year 10 (43.3%).
Postsecondary Graduation Location (Cramer’s V: 0.158, 0.191, 0.162) A weak but remarkably stable and substantively compelling association across all three time points. Regional graduates show the strongest and most persistent attachment to meaningful regional employment at every time point, with rates consistently 10-15 percentage points higher than other graduation location groups by years 5 and 10. PS grad outside MN graduates show the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record, reaching 54.8% by year 10 — the highest rate observed for any subgroup across all variables examined.
Postsecondary Graduation Institution Sector (Cramer’s V: 0.149, 0.161, 0.125) A weak but consistent association across all three time points. Public 2-year institution graduates show the strongest and most persistent regional employment attachment, with meaningful regional employment rates above 35% at both years 5 and 10. Private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the strongest tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. These findings directly reinforce the graduation location results, as regional institutions are predominantly public 2-year institutions.

Overall Pattern The postsecondary variables collectively tell a coherent and substantively important story for regional workforce development. In the short term, postsecondary attendance and graduation status dominate the picture, largely reflecting structural relationships with the outcome categories. As these structural effects dissipate over time, a clear and consistent pattern emerges — the type, level, and location of postsecondary education shapes not whether individuals are meaningfully employed, but where they are employed. Sub-baccalaureate credentials, public 2-year institutions, and regional postsecondary graduation are all consistently associated with stronger regional workforce attachment, while higher credential levels, 4-year institutions, and out-of-state graduation are associated with employment outside the region or out of state entirely. The finding that graduating from a regional postsecondary institution is among the strongest predictors of long-term regional workforce retention has direct and actionable implications for regional higher education and workforce development policy.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
attended.ps
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between postsecondary attendance (No = did not attend, Yes = attended) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Postsecondary attendance shows the strongest association of any variable examined at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.698), reflecting its near-definitional relationship with the attending postsecondary outcome category, and weakens considerably over time as postsecondary attendance becomes a historical rather than current status.
Year 1 At one year post-graduation, postsecondary attendance shows a very strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.698). This is largely driven by the structural relationship between the variable and the outcome — virtually all individuals in the attending postsecondary outcome category attended postsecondary (26,953 of 26,954), making this association near-tautological at year 1. Among those who did not attend postsecondary, the dominant outcomes are not meaningful employment (39.7%) and no Minnesota employment record (31.6%), with meaningful regional employment accounting for 21.7%. Among those who did attend postsecondary, 75.8% are still attending at year 1, with the remaining 24.2% distributed across workforce participation outcomes. (χ²=23222.936, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.698, p<0.001)
Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens substantially (Cramer’s V=0.295) as postsecondary attendance becomes a historical characteristic rather than a current status. Non-attendees show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (26.2% vs 22.8%) and notably higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (36.3% vs 19.6%), while postsecondary attendees show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (19.8% vs 13.0%) and postsecondary attendance (22.1% vs 0.0%). The divergence in no Minnesota employment record between the two groups is particularly notable, suggesting non-attendees are considerably more likely to leave Minnesota entirely by year 5. (χ²=3064.364, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.295, p<0.001)
Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens considerably to a weak effect (Cramer’s V=0.108). Rates of meaningful regional employment converge almost entirely between non-attendees (25.2%) and attendees (24.7%). Postsecondary attendees show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (25.9% vs 15.6%), while non-attendees show a higher rate of no Minnesota employment record (40.3% vs 35.0%). The convergence in meaningful regional employment rates suggests that by year 10 postsecondary attendance history has little bearing on whether individuals are employed in the region, though it continues to distinguish between Minnesota and out-of-state employment outcomes. (χ²=234.106, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.108, p<0.001)

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
ps.grad
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between postsecondary graduation (No = did not graduate, Y = graduated) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Note that individuals currently attending postsecondary are excluded from this analysis as their graduation status is not yet determined. Postsecondary graduation shows a very strong association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with a pattern that closely mirrors postsecondary attendance but with important distinctions in the magnitude and direction of effects.
Year 1 At one year post-graduation, postsecondary graduation shows a very strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.633). As with postsecondary attendance, this is substantially driven by the structural relationship between graduation status and the attending postsecondary outcome category — postsecondary graduates show an 83.7% rate of still attending postsecondary at year 1, reflecting those who have completed one credential and are pursuing another. Non-graduates show substantially higher rates of meaningful regional employment (19.3% vs 7.4%), not meaningful employment (31.2% vs 4.7%), and no Minnesota employment record (21.3% vs 1.7%), while graduates show markedly lower rates across all non-postsecondary outcomes at this early stage. (χ²=18207.912, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.633, p<0.001)
Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens considerably but remains moderate (Cramer’s V=0.330). Non-graduates show higher rates of meaningful regional employment (25.7% vs 22.0%) and notably higher rates of no Minnesota employment record (30.7% vs 17.9%), while postsecondary graduates show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (20.7% vs 15.1%) and postsecondary attendance (26.2% vs 4.5%). The divergence in no Minnesota employment record between the two groups is particularly striking — non-graduates are nearly twice as likely to have no Minnesota employment record as graduates, suggesting postsecondary graduation is strongly associated with remaining employed in Minnesota. (χ²=3791.997, df=4, Cramer’s V=0.330, p<0.001)
Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens to a weak effect (Cramer’s V=0.130). Rates of meaningful regional employment converge almost entirely between non-graduates (24.7%) and graduates (24.8%). Postsecondary graduates show a notably higher rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (27.9% vs 17.9%), while non-graduates show a higher rate of no Minnesota employment record (38.6% vs 34.3%) and not meaningful employment (18.7% vs 13.0%). The convergence in meaningful regional employment rates mirrors the pattern observed for postsecondary attendance, suggesting that by year 10 postsecondary graduation has little bearing on whether individuals are employed in the region but continues to distinguish between the quality and location of employment outcomes. (χ²=341.123, df=3, Cramer’s V=0.130, p<0.001)
Summary Postsecondary graduation shows a very strong association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.633), largely driven by structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category, that weakens to a weak effect by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.130). The most substantively important finding is the persistent and growing advantage of postsecondary graduates in achieving meaningful Minnesota employment across all time points, while non-graduates show stronger attachment to regional employment and considerably higher rates of no Minnesota employment record. By year 10 meaningful regional employment rates converge entirely between the two groups, suggesting that postsecondary graduation shapes where people work rather than whether they are meaningfully employed in the region.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
ps.grad.InstitutionSector
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between the sector of postsecondary institution from which individuals graduated (1=Public 4-year, 2=Private not-for-profit 4-year, 4=Public 2-year, 11=Private other) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. This analysis is restricted to postsecondary graduates and those currently attending, excluding individuals who never attended or did not graduate. Institution sector shows a weak but consistent association across all three time points, with substantively meaningful differences in the type of employment achieved across sectors.
Year 1 At one year post-graduation, institution sector shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.149). The overwhelming majority of individuals across all sectors are still attending postsecondary at year 1, ranging from 70.5% for public 2-year graduates to 94.3% for public 4-year graduates, reflecting the expected pattern that 4-year institution graduates are more likely to be pursuing additional credentials. Among those in the workforce, public 2-year graduates show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (15.3%), more than double the rate of public 4-year graduates (2.6%) and private not-for-profit 4-year graduates (0.9%), suggesting that 2-year institution graduates enter regional employment much more quickly. (χ²=2371.287, df=16, Cramer’s V=0.149, p<0.001)
Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.161) but the patterns become considerably more interpretable as postsecondary attendance rates decline. Public 2-year graduates show by far the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (35.6%), more than double that of public 4-year graduates (16.2%) and private not-for-profit 4-year graduates (8.9%). Conversely, private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (28.5%), followed by public 4-year graduates (20.8%). Private other graduates show notably lower rates of no Minnesota employment record (8.3%) compared to all other sectors, while private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest rate (24.2%), suggesting considerably higher out-of-state migration among graduates of private 4-year institutions. (χ²=2046.570, df=16, Cramer’s V=0.161, p<0.001)
Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.125). Public 2-year graduates continue to show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (35.8%), more than three times that of private not-for-profit 4-year graduates (11.2%) and substantially higher than public 4-year graduates (21.0%) and private other graduates (24.6%). Public 4-year and private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show similar rates of meaningful Minnesota employment (30.7% and 33.7% respectively), while public 2-year graduates show the lowest rate (20.8%). Private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (40.3%), while public 2-year graduates show the lowest (28.7%). (χ²=519.468, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.125, p<0.001)
Summary Postsecondary graduation institution sector shows a weak but consistent association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (Cramer’s V=0.149, 0.161, 0.125). Despite the modest effect sizes, the findings are substantively compelling and tell a clear story — public 2-year institution graduates show the strongest and most persistent attachment to regional employment across all time points, with meaningful regional employment rates remaining above 35% at both years 5 and 10. In contrast, private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the strongest tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment and the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record, suggesting these graduates are the most geographically mobile and least likely to remain in the region. Public 4-year graduates occupy a middle position, showing moderate rates of both regional and Minnesota employment. These findings have direct implications for regional workforce development, suggesting that 2-year institution pathways are particularly important for regional talent retention.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
highest.cred.level
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between highest credential level attained (No college, Some college, Less than Associate Degree, Associate Degree, Attending ps, Bachelor Degree, Master Degree or higher) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. Highest credential level shows a strong association at year 1 that weakens considerably over time, with substantively meaningful patterns in how credential level shapes the type and location of employment achieved.
Year 1 At one year post-graduation, highest credential level shows a strong association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.378). At this early stage the results are heavily influenced by the structural relationship between credential level and the attending postsecondary outcome category, with postsecondary attendance rates increasing monotonically with credential level — from 0% for no college to 93.0% for master degree or higher. Among those in the workforce, a clear gradient emerges in meaningful regional employment, declining with higher credential levels — from 21.7% for no college to 1.9% for master degree or higher. Conversely, no college individuals show the highest rates of not meaningful employment (39.7%) and no Minnesota employment record (31.6%). (χ²=27300.114, df=24, Cramer’s V=0.378, p<0.001)
Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association weakens to a moderate effect (Cramer’s V=0.246) as credential attainment becomes more fully realized. A notable and substantively important pattern emerges — individuals with less than associate degree (35.1%) and associate degree (32.1%) show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment, considerably higher than bachelor degree (16.5%) and master degree or higher (8.1%) holders. The inverse is true for meaningful Minnesota employment, where bachelor degree (23.5%) and master degree or higher (11.8%) holders show lower regional but higher out-of-region employment rates. Master degree or higher individuals show the highest rate of still attending postsecondary (72.9%), reflecting ongoing graduate education. No college individuals show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (36.3%). (χ²=8561.286, df=24, Cramer’s V=0.246, p<0.001)
Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association weakens to a weak effect (Cramer’s V=0.111). The patterns observed at year 5 persist and clarify further. Less than associate degree (34.4%) and associate degree (33.1%) holders continue to show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment, while bachelor degree (31.3%) and master degree or higher (29.1%) holders show the highest rates of meaningful Minnesota employment. Master degree or higher holders show the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (43.3%), suggesting the highest credential level is associated with the greatest geographic mobility and lowest regional retention. No college and some college individuals show relatively similar distributions across outcome categories, suggesting that without any credential attainment the long-term workforce trajectory is similar regardless of whether some postsecondary was attempted. (χ²=746.232, df=15, Cramer’s V=0.111, p<0.001)
Summary Highest credential level shows a strong association with workforce participation outcomes at year 1 (Cramer’s V=0.378) that weakens to a weak effect by year 10 (Cramer’s V=0.111). The most substantively important finding is the clear and persistent inverse relationship between credential level and regional workforce attachment — sub-baccalaureate credential holders (less than associate and associate degree) show the strongest regional employment rates across all time points, while bachelor and master degree holders show increasing tendencies toward meaningful Minnesota employment and out-of-state migration. This pattern is consistent with and reinforces findings from the institution sector analysis, where public 2-year graduates showed the strongest regional attachment, and aligns with the broader pattern observed across academic achievement variables suggesting that higher educational attainment is associated with employment outside the region in Southwest Minnesota.

One year after graduatingFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
text:
ps.grad.location
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between the location of postsecondary graduation (Did not graduate ps, PS grad MN, PS grad outside MN, PS grad region) and workforce participation outcomes at 1, 5, and 10 years after high school graduation. This variable is uniquely relevant to the regional workforce development focus of this study, as it directly captures whether individuals pursued and completed postsecondary education within the Southwest Minnesota region, elsewhere in Minnesota, or outside the state. The association is weak but consistent across all three time points, with substantively compelling patterns in regional workforce attachment by graduation location.
Year 1 At one year post-graduation, graduation location shows a weak association with workforce participation outcomes (Cramer’s V=0.158). Among those in the workforce rather than attending postsecondary, regional graduates show the strongest attachment to meaningful regional employment (56.2%), substantially higher than PS grad MN (35.5%), PS grad outside MN (38.2%), and did not graduate ps (24.6%). Did not graduate ps individuals show the highest rates of not meaningful employment (40.0%) and no Minnesota employment record (27.3%), while regional graduates show the lowest rate of no Minnesota employment record (6.2%), suggesting that graduating from a regional institution is strongly associated with immediate regional workforce engagement. (χ²=1514.441, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.158, p<0.001)
Year 5 By five years post-graduation the association strengthens modestly (Cramer’s V=0.191). Regional graduates continue to show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (42.4%), considerably higher than PS grad MN (23.9%), PS grad outside MN (23.9%), and did not graduate ps (26.9%). PS grad MN graduates show the highest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (40.9%), while PS grad outside MN graduates show a strikingly high rate of no Minnesota employment record (47.4%), suggesting that graduating outside Minnesota is strongly associated with long-term out-of-state retention. Regional graduates show the lowest rate of no Minnesota employment record (13.4%), reinforcing the strong regional retention associated with regional graduation. (χ²=3181.250, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.191, p<0.001)
Year 10 At ten years post-graduation the association remains weak (Cramer’s V=0.162). Regional graduates continue to show the highest rate of meaningful regional employment (36.9%), significantly higher than PS grad MN (20.4%), PS grad outside MN (18.0%), and did not graduate ps (24.8%). PS grad MN graduates show the highest rate of meaningful Minnesota employment (38.0%), while PS grad outside MN graduates maintain the highest rate of no Minnesota employment record (54.8%) — the highest rate of out-of-state retention observed for any subgroup across all variables examined. Regional graduates show the lowest rate of no Minnesota employment record (23.2%), the lowest of any graduation location group across all time points. (χ²=1582.788, df=9, Cramer’s V=0.162, p<0.001)
Summary Postsecondary graduation location shows a weak but consistent and substantively compelling association with workforce participation outcomes across all three time points (Cramer’s V=0.158, 0.191, 0.162). Despite the modest effect sizes, the findings tell a clear and policy-relevant story. Regional graduates show the strongest and most persistent attachment to meaningful regional employment at every time point, with rates consistently 10-15 percentage points higher than other graduation location groups by years 5 and 10. Conversely, graduating outside Minnesota is associated with the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record at all time points, reaching 54.8% by year 10. PS grad MN graduates occupy a middle position, showing strong meaningful Minnesota employment but moderate regional attachment. These findings strongly suggest that the location of postsecondary graduation is one of the most direct predictors of long-term regional workforce retention, with regional institutions playing a critical role in keeping graduates employed in Southwest Minnesota.

One year after graduatingFive years after graduatingTen years after graduating

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
section: Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1
text:
Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V at Year 1

PS Attendance (Cramer’s V: 0.698, 0.295, 0.108) The strongest association at year 1 but substantially inflated by structural overlap with the attending postsecondary outcome category. Association weakens considerably over time. Long-term finding: non-attendees show stronger regional attachment while attendees gravitate toward meaningful Minnesota employment.
PS Graduation (Cramer’s V: 0.633, 0.330, 0.130) Similarly inflated at year 1 by structural overlap. Weakens considerably over time. Long-term finding: graduates show higher rates of meaningful Minnesota employment while non-graduates show stronger regional attachment and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record.
ACT Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.430, 0.225, 0.137) The strongest non-structurally-inflated predictor and the most meaningful high school accomplishment variable. Non-ACT students show strong and persistent regional workforce attachment across all time points, while ACT takers are strongly associated with postsecondary attendance and subsequent out-of-region employment.
Highest Credential Level (Cramer’s V: 0.378, 0.246, 0.111) A strong association at year 1 that weakens over time. A clear inverse relationship emerges between credential level and regional attachment — sub-baccalaureate credential holders show the strongest regional employment rates while bachelor and master degree holders increasingly gravitate toward out-of-region employment and out-of-state migration.
PSEO Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.370, 0.165, 0.087) Strong at year 1, largely reflecting direct relationship with postsecondary attendance. PSEO participants show persistent tendency toward meaningful Minnesota employment over regional employment, suggesting broader geographic mobility associated with early college experience.
Economic Status (Cramer’s V: 0.285, 0.127, 0.094) The strongest demographic predictor, particularly at year 1. Economically disadvantaged students enter the workforce earlier but into less stable employment, while non- disadvantaged students pursue postsecondary at higher rates. Differences narrow considerably by years 5 and 10.
Special Education Status (Cramer’s V: 0.265, 0.149, 0.097) A moderate association at year 1 that weakens over time. Special education students enter the workforce earlier and show a persistent tendency toward regional over Minnesota employment across all time points.
AP Exam Participation (Cramer’s V: 0.217, 0.146, 0.089) Moderate at year 1, weakening over time. Mirrors the ACT participation pattern — AP exam takers show persistent tendency toward out-of-region employment while non-takers show stronger regional attachment.
English Learner Status (Cramer’s V: 0.214, 0.086, 0.059) Moderate at year 1, weakening considerably over time. English learners enter the workforce earlier but into less stable situations, with persistent challenges in achieving meaningful Minnesota employment through year 10.
Non-English Home Language (Cramer’s V: 0.178, 0.058, 0.047) Closely mirrors English learner status throughout, consistent with the strong overlap between these variables identified during MCA. Slightly weaker associations at all time points.
PS Grad Location (Cramer’s V: 0.158, 0.191, 0.162) A weak but remarkably stable association that is among the most substantively important findings of the entire analysis. Regional graduates show the strongest and most persistent regional employment attachment at every time point, while graduating outside Minnesota is associated with the highest rates of no Minnesota employment record, reaching 54.8% by year 10.
PS Grad Institution Sector (Cramer’s V: 0.149, 0.161, 0.125) Weak but stable across all time points. Public 2-year institution graduates show the strongest regional attachment while private not-for-profit 4-year graduates show the highest geographic mobility. Directly reinforces the graduation location findings.
Race/Ethnicity (Cramer’s V: 0.108, 0.044, 0.047) Weak and declining association. Notable patterns include persistent workforce challenges for AI students and higher rates of no Minnesota employment record among all minority groups at year 10. Small sample sizes for minority groups warrant cautious interpretation.
Gender (Cramer’s V: 0.097, 0.093, 0.056) Consistently weak across all time points. Females more likely to pursue postsecondary immediately after graduation while males transition more quickly into regional employment, though differences narrow considerably by year 10.
EDR (Cramer’s V: 0.079, 0.110, 0.124) Weak but uniquely strengthening over time. EDR 6E Southwest Central students show a growing advantage in meaningful Minnesota employment while EDR 8 Southwest students show increasing rates of no Minnesota employment record, suggesting growing geographic divergence within the region.
CTE Achievement (Cramer’s V: 0.075, 0.090, 0.088) The weakest categorical predictor but with a substantively important and stable pattern. CTE Concentrators or Completors show the highest rates of meaningful regional employment at every time point, with No CTE students showing strikingly high rates of no Minnesota employment record by year 10 (43.8%).
Community Demographic Description (Cramer’s V: 0.057, 0.072, 0.079) The weakest categorical predictor overall but also strengthening over time. Urban/town/rural mix students increasingly gravitate toward out-of-region employment while town/rural mix and entirely rural students show stronger regional attachment.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
section: Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared at Year 1
text:
Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared at Year 1

MCA Math (η²: 0.168, 0.025, 0.017) The strongest continuous predictor at year 1 with a large effect size, the largest observed for any continuous variable. A distinctive early pattern shows lower Math scores associated with both not meaningful employment and no Minnesota employment record — unique among MCA subjects. By year 10 the meaningful regional employment group shows the lowest Math scores of all groups.
MCA Reading (η²: 0.129, 0.023, 0.057) The second strongest continuous predictor at year 1 with a large effect size, notable for its unique trajectory — weakening to a small effect at year 5 before strengthening to a medium effect at year 10. The only continuous variable to strengthen at year 10, suggesting Reading achievement becomes increasingly predictive of out-of-region employment over time.
ACT Composite Score (η²: 0.093, 0.038, 0.028) A moderate effect at year 1 weakening to small over time. The attending postsecondary group shows substantially higher scores than all workforce-engaged groups, and the meaningful regional employment group shows progressively lower scores relative to other groups over time.
MCA Science (η²: 0.085, 0.033, 0.019) A large effect at year 1 weakening to small over time. Results should be interpreted with caution given the voluntary and self-selected nature of Science MCA participation. Pattern mirrors Reading and Math, with regional employment consistently showing the lowest Science scores.
Total CTE Courses Taken (η²: 0.032, 0.027, 0.028) A small but remarkably stable effect across all three time points, unique among continuous variables. The meaningful regional employment group shows the highest number of CTE courses taken at every time point, with the gap between regional and out-of-region employment groups growing over time.
Average Wages as Percentage of State Average (η²: 0.010, 0.004, 0.006) A negligible effect throughout, explaining at most 1% of variance. Despite very small effect sizes, regional employment consistently shows higher relative wages than meaningful Minnesota employment, suggesting competitive regional wages relative to the broader state labor market.
Average Unemployment Rate (η²: 0.005, 0.015, 0.032) The weakest predictor at year 1 but growing to a small effect by year 10. Individuals with no Minnesota employment record consistently experience lower unemployment rates, likely reflecting out-of-state migration to stronger labor markets.

title: Local Employment Cross-tabs
section: Key Themes Across All Variable Groups
text:
Key Themes Across All Variable Groups
Three overarching themes emerge from the analysis:
The first is a persistent academic achievement gradient in geographic mobility. Across virtually every academic achievement variable — ACT participation, AP exam, PSEO, MCA scores, ACT composite, and credential level — higher achievement is consistently associated with postsecondary attendance and subsequent employment outside the region. This pattern strengthens over time and is one of the most robust findings of the entire analysis.
The second is the importance of CTE and vocational pathways for regional retention. In direct contrast to the academic achievement pattern, CTE engagement variables — CTE achievement, total CTE courses taken, and by extension public 2-year institution graduation and sub-baccalaureate credentials — are consistently associated with stronger regional workforce attachment across all time points. This divergence between academic and CTE pathways has direct implications for regional workforce development policy.
The third is the critical role of postsecondary geography. Where individuals attend and graduate from postsecondary education is among the most stable and policy-relevant predictors of long-term regional workforce retention. Regional postsecondary graduation is associated with the strongest regional employment attachment while out-of-state graduation is associated with the highest rates of permanent out-of-state migration. This finding points directly to the strategic importance of regional postsecondary institutions for Southwest Minnesota workforce development.

write_csv(master.after.ct, "Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-after-ct.csv")

In the dataset there are 2,121,377 observations along with 15 columns. Each observation does not represent a unique, unduplicated individual, but rather a unique status for the individual. For example, one individual can have multiple observations if they changed schools, for each school year, or if their economic status changed in the middle of a school year.
Next we need to create a dataset that only has students who graduated from a high school located in the Southwest research region - EDRs 6W, 6E, and 8. To do that, we will need to join up with the organization spreadsheet.


k12.org.original <- read_csv('/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/CRPD 2 (angelique.workman@state.mn.us)/K12Organization_cleaned.csv') %>%
  distinct(K12OrganizationID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  drop_na(K12OrganizationID) %>%
  mutate(K12OrganizationID = as.integer(K12OrganizationID)) %>%
  group_by(NCESID) %>%
  fill(SiteCountyCode, .direction = "downup") %>%
  ungroup()

This dataset provides each school institution as of June 29, 2024. This isn’t a list of each school but rather a list of each K12OrganizationID. Each school will have multiple K12OrganizationID. I’m not sure why and this would be something to ask Meredith.
In addition, there are a lot of county codes missing. So I’m going to use the listed city to try and clean that up.
My goal is to join my standardized region and county code data with this document which will then be joined to the master enrollment document. Unfortunately the main data does not include county name but rather their own code for county. So I will join their codes with the county names and then use my counties.regions data to bring together the remaining regions.


SiteCountyCode <- read_csv("~/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Research/FY25-27/FY26/OHE Journey to meaningful employment/Data/SiteCountyCodes.csv")

k12.org.master <- k12.org.original %>%
  left_join(SiteCountyCode[,c(1,2)], by = c("SiteCountyCode" = "Code")) %>%
  drop_na(SiteCountyCode) %>%
  rename(county.name = `Code definition`) %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name) %>%
  mutate(county.name = str_replace(county.name, " County", ""),
         county.name = str_replace(county.name, "Saint Louis", "St. Louis")) %>%
  filter(county.name != "Unknown") %>%
  select(K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name) %>%
  left_join(counties.regions.1, by = c("county.name" = "Name")) %>%
  select(-Dem_RUCA, -mif, -edr.simple)

The dataset now has 216,240 instances that provide a number of characteristics about an individuals enrollment.
My goal is to end up with a dataset where each observation (row) represents a unique (unduplicated) individual that’s tied to the school from which they graduated. Here’s how I will do that.

PersonID: change to integer
K12OrganizationID: change to integer.
K12EnrTimeID: Change so it’s only the year
Grade: eliminate - unimportant since we are mostly concerned about whether they graduated.
EconomicStatusIndicator: I will change this to two categories. Whether they were ever eligible for reduce priced or free meal will be coded as 1 (eligible = codes 1, 2, 4, 5) at any point in the dataset. The remaining will be coded as 0.
FederalRaceIndicator: eliminate - I’m going to use the RaceEthnicity column instead.
PSEO columns: I’m most concerned about whether they participated in PSEO or not. A new column will be created indicated whether there was a “Y” coded for either the “PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator” or “PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator” columns at any point in the dataset. The two original columns will be eliminated.
StatusEnd: I will only include individuals that have StatusEnd coded as an 8 or 9.
Gender: included and not changed.
SpecialEdStatus: I will split this into two categories. The first is that a student did not require special education services (codes 1-2) at any point in the dataset. The other category will be that they require special education (codes 4 - 9) at any point in the dataset.
Limited English: keep with no changes.
Primary language: I’m going to create two categories. One being that English is the primary language while the other will be that English is NOT the primary language at any point in the dataset.
Homeless: Keep with no changes.

We first need to create a list of all individuals with the StatusEnd code of 8 or 9 (graduated). In addition, we need to remove any student that graduated before 2008. This is because we will not have their full Sophmore - Senior records which is especially important when attempting to identify whether the number of CTE courses taken predicts migration.
The other element I want to add is a pre-covid and post-covid variable. All individuals whose grad year is before 2020 will be labeled pre-covid and all others wil lbe post-covid.
This “graduates list” will be the list on which we will bind all the other variables.


graduates <- enrollment.org.sw %>%
  filter(StatusEnd %in% c(8,9)) %>%
  mutate(grad.year = str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4),
         grad.year = as.integer(grad.year),
         grad.year.covid = ifelse(grad.year > 2019, "Post-covid grad", "Pre-covid grad")) %>%
  select(PersonID, K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, grad.year, grad.year.covid, Gender, LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator, HomelessIndicator, county.name, countyfp, Dem_Desc, edr, planning.region) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  filter(grad.year > 2007) 


Next we need to create new columns for the variables that will indicate a certain status has been met at any point in the dataset for each PersonID. For example, we want a column that indicates a PersonID was enrolled in PSEO at some point in the dataset. The variables that need a new column created are;

EconomicStatusIndicator
PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator & PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator
SpecialEdStatus
HomeprimaryLanguage



# Lets start with economic status indicator
k12.enr.esi.indicator <- enrollment.org.sw %>%
  select(PersonID, EconomicStatusIndicator) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  mutate(economic.status.code = ifelse(EconomicStatusIndicator %in% c(1,2,4,5), 1, 0),
         economic.status.code.sum = sum(economic.status.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(economic.status = ifelse(economic.status.code.sum > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  select(PersonID, economic.status)

# PSEO

k12.enr.pseo.indicator <- enrollment.org.sw%>%
  select(PersonID, PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator, PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator) %>%
  mutate(PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator = ifelse(PostSecondaryEnrollmentOptionsIndicator == "Y", 1, 0),
         PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator = ifelse(PSEOConcurrentEnrollmentIndicator == "Y", 1, 0)) %>%
  gather(key = "pseo.type", value = "indicator", 2:3) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(pseo = sum(indicator)) %>%
  mutate(pseo.participant = ifelse(pseo > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(PersonID, pseo.participant)

# Special Ed status

k12.enr.spced.indicator <- enrollment.org.sw %>%
  select(PersonID, SpecialEdStatus) %>%
  mutate(SpecialEdStatus.code = ifelse(SpecialEdStatus %in% c(4,5,6,7,8,9), 1, 0)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(SpecialEdStatus.sum = sum(SpecialEdStatus.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(SpecialEdStatus = ifelse(SpecialEdStatus.sum > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  select(PersonID, SpecialEdStatus)

# Home Primary Language

k12.enr.homeprimlang.indicator <- enrollment.org.sw %>%
  select(PersonID, HomePrimaryLanguage) %>%
  mutate(HomePrimaryLanguage.code = ifelse(HomePrimaryLanguage == 11, 0, 1)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarise(HomePrimaryLanguage.sum = sum(HomePrimaryLanguage.code)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(non.english.home = ifelse(HomePrimaryLanguage.sum > 0, 1, 0)) %>%
  select(PersonID, non.english.home)

# Race

k12.enr.race <- enrollment.org.sw %>%
  select(PersonID, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  mutate(RaceEthnicity = ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 0, "Unknown",
                                ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 1, "AI",
                                       ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 2, "Asian/PI",
                                              ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 3, "Hispanic",
                                                     ifelse(RaceEthnicity == 4, "Black", "White"))))),
         RaceEthnicity = as.factor(RaceEthnicity)) %>%
  distinct(PersonID, RaceEthnicity) %>%
  mutate(code = ifelse(RaceEthnicity == "Unknown", 0, 1)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  filter(code == max(code)) %>%
  ungroup %>%
  distinct(PersonID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  select(PersonID, RaceEthnicity)


Now we can combine the list of graduates with the status indicators.


k12.enrollment.master <- graduates %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.esi.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.pseo.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.spced.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.homeprimlang.indicator, by = "PersonID") %>%
  left_join(k12.enr.race, by = "PersonID") %>%
  select(PersonID, K12OrganizationID, OrganizationName, county.name, countyfp, Dem_Desc, edr, planning.region, grad.year, grad.year.covid, Gender, LimitedEnglishProficiencyIndicator, HomelessIndicator, economic.status, pseo.participant, SpecialEdStatus, non.english.home, RaceEthnicity)

Summary of K12Enrollment data
Below is the percentage of total observations that;

Were eligible for free or reduced lunch at some point in the dataset
Are Female
Have been homeless at some point in the dataset
Had limited english proficiency at some point in the dataset
Had non english speakders in home at some point in the dataset

Pct non white

As expected, nearly half (49.2%) of the observations are female. Over a third (37%) of the observations have been eligible for free or reduced lunch at some point in the dataset. Also, over a third (33.4%) of observations participated in a PSEO course. There is a large percentage identifying as non-white with 34.1%, while 13.8% were categorized as needing special education, 10% with a non-english speaker in the home, 2.9% with limited english proficiency and 0.5% homeless.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Summary
Cross-tabulation analysis and one-way ANOVA examined associations between independent variables and postsecondary attendance outcomes (Did not attend PS, Attended - did not grad, Attended - grad) for 47,189 individuals. Variables are presented in two separate ranked lists by effect size measure.
Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V

took.ACT (Cramer’s V=0.443) — The strongest categorical predictor. ACT takers show a graduation rate of 67.3% compared to 26.6% for non-takers, with 53.6% of non-takers not attending postsecondary at all.
pseo.participant (Cramer’s V=0.401) — A strong predictor, noting the near-tautological nature of the relationship. PSEO participants show a graduation rate of 76.3% compared to 36.5% for non-participants, with 42.1% of non-participants not attending postsecondary.
economic.status (Cramer’s V=0.299) — Joint strongest demographic predictor alongside special education status. Economically disadvantaged students show a graduation rate of 34.6% compared to 64.4% for non-disadvantaged students, with 42.4% not attending postsecondary.
special.ed.status (Cramer’s V=0.299) — Joint strongest demographic predictor. Special education students show a graduation rate of 21.7% compared to 58.3% for non-special education students, with 59.6% not attending postsecondary.
ap.exam (Cramer’s V=0.230) — A moderate association. AP exam participants show the highest graduation rate of any categorical subgroup examined (79.4%) and the lowest rate of not attending (7.0%).
english.learner (Cramer’s V=0.224) — A moderate association. English learners show a graduation rate of 24.0% compared to 56.3%, with 57.0% not attending postsecondary.
non.english.home (Cramer’s V=0.168) — A weak to moderate association closely mirroring english.learner. Students from non-English speaking homes show a graduation rate of 28.9% compared to 55.7%, with 48.5% not attending.
race.ethnicity (Cramer’s V=0.148) — A weak to moderate association. AI students show the highest rate of not attending (48.6%) while White students show the lowest (22.1%). Asian/PI and Black students show relatively higher graduation rates (42.9% and 39.5% respectively).
gender (Cramer’s V=0.129) — A weak association. Females show a notably higher graduation rate (59.6% vs 47.0%) and lower rate of not attending (23.2% vs 32.7%).
dem.desc (Cramer’s V=0.053) — A very weak association. Entirely rural students show the highest graduation rate (60.9%) and lowest rate of not attending (21.5%).
cte.achievement (Cramer’s V=0.041) — A very weak and inverse association. Higher CTE engagement is modestly associated with lower postsecondary graduation rates, consistent with the workforce pathway sorting mechanism.
edr (Cramer’s V=0.023) — A negligible association. Geographic subregion within Southwest Minnesota has virtually no bearing on postsecondary attendance outcomes.

Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared

MCA Reading (η²=0.106) — The strongest continuous predictor with a medium effect. Graduates score significantly higher than non-completers and did not attend PS individuals score significantly lower than both attended groups, with a gap of 0.721 between graduates and non-attendees.
ACT Composite Score (η²=0.094) — A small to medium effect, restricted to ACT takers. The gap between graduates and did not attend PS individuals is very large (estimate=-3.883), reflecting the powerful sorting role of academic achievement in postsecondary pathway selection.
MCA Science (η²=0.088) — A small to medium effect, noting the voluntary and self-selected nature of participation. Pattern mirrors Reading and Math with graduates scoring significantly higher than all other groups.
MCA Math (η²=0.079) — A small to medium effect closely mirroring MCA Reading and Science. Graduates score significantly higher than non-completers and did not attend PS individuals score significantly lower than both attended groups.
avg.unemployment.rate (η²=0.007) — A negligible effect consistent with the opportunity cost hypothesis. Did not attend PS individuals experienced significantly lower average unemployment rates, suggesting favorable local labor market conditions are associated with direct workforce entry.
total.cte.courses.taken (η²=0.008) — A negligible effect. Graduates show significantly fewer CTE courses than non-completers and did not attend PS individuals, further reinforcing the inverse relationship between CTE engagement and postsecondary attendance.
avg.wages.pct.state (η²=0.006) — A negligible effect. Local wage levels explain less than one percent of variance in postsecondary attendance outcomes.

Overall Pattern High school accomplishment variables — particularly took.ACT, pseo.participant, and MCA scores — are the strongest predictors of postsecondary attendance outcomes, considerably stronger than demographic or geographic variables. Among demographic variables, economic status and special education status are the joint strongest predictors, with both showing that disadvantaged groups are primarily distinguished by much higher rates of not attending rather than higher rates of attending without completing, suggesting access rather than completion is the primary barrier. A consistent inverse relationship between CTE engagement and postsecondary attendance across both CTE achievement and total CTE courses taken, combined with negligible geographic effects, reinforces the pathway sorting mechanism in which CTE engagement is associated with direct regional workforce entry rather than postsecondary pursuit. Local labor market conditions show negligible associations, suggesting that individual academic preparation and demographic characteristics are far more powerful determinants of postsecondary attendance than local economic conditions in this regional context.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Demographics
Here are the following variables;

Demographics

Gender
economic.status
RaceEthnicity
SpecialEdStatus
english.learner
non.english.home



Summary
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between demographic variables and postsecondary attendance outcomes (Did not attend PS, Attended - did not grad, Attended - grad) for 47,189 individuals. Variables are ranked by Cramer’s V from strongest to weakest association.
Ranked by Cramer’s V:
1. Economic Status (Cramer’s V=0.299) The strongest demographic predictor of postsecondary attendance. Non-disadvantaged students show a graduation rate of 64.4% compared to just 34.6% for economically disadvantaged students, while 42.4% of economically disadvantaged students did not attend postsecondary compared to 19.4% of non-disadvantaged students. Economic disadvantage is clearly a substantial barrier to both accessing and completing postsecondary education.
2. Special Education Status (Cramer’s V=0.299) Equally strong as economic status. Special education students show a dramatically lower graduation rate (21.7% vs 58.3%) and a strikingly high rate of not attending postsecondary (59.6% vs 22.9%). Notably, the attended but did not graduate rates are virtually identical between the two groups (18.8% vs 18.8%), suggesting that when special education students do attend they face similar completion challenges to non-special education students but are far less likely to attempt postsecondary in the first place.
3. English Learner Status (Cramer’s V=0.224) A moderate association. English learners show a much lower graduation rate (24.0% vs 56.3%) and a much higher rate of not attending (57.0% vs 24.9%), while attended but did not graduate rates are nearly identical between the two groups (19.0% vs 18.8%), mirroring the pattern observed for special education status.
4. Non-English Home Language (Cramer’s V=0.168) A weak to moderate association closely mirroring the English learner pattern. Students from non-English speaking homes show lower graduation rates (28.9% vs 55.7%) and higher rates of not attending (48.5% vs 25.9%).
5. Gender (Cramer’s V=0.129) A weak association. Females show a notably higher graduation rate (59.6% vs 47.0%) and lower rate of not attending (23.2% vs 32.7%), consistent with well-established national patterns of female postsecondary attainment advantage. The gender gap in graduation rates is notably larger in this dataset than typically observed nationally.
6. Race/Ethnicity (Cramer’s V=0.148) A weak to moderate association with notable variation across groups. AI students show the highest rate of not attending (48.6%) while White students show the lowest (22.1%). Asian/PI and Black students show relatively higher graduation rates (42.9% and 39.5% respectively) compared to AI (24.7%) and Hispanic (26.5%) students. Small sample sizes for minority groups warrant cautious interpretation.
Summary Economic status and special education status are the joint strongest demographic predictors of postsecondary attendance outcomes (both Cramer’s V=0.299), with both variables showing large differences in graduation rates and not attending rates. A consistent and important pattern across economic status, special education, English learner, and non-English home language variables is that disadvantaged groups are primarily distinguished by much higher rates of not attending rather than higher rates of attending without graduating — the attended but did not graduate rates are nearly identical across all disadvantaged and non-disadvantaged groups — suggesting that access rather than completion is the primary barrier for these groups. Gender shows a weak but substantively meaningful association with females showing considerably higher graduation rates than males.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Characteristics
Here are the variables we will be testing;

High school characteristics

Dem_Desc
edr
avg.unemp.rate
avg.wage.pct.state




Summary
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis and one-way ANOVA examined the relationship between high school characteristics and local labor market variables and postsecondary attendance outcomes (Did not attend PS, Attended - did not grad, Attended - grad) for 47,189 individuals. Categorical variables are ranked by Cramer’s V and continuous variables by eta-squared.
Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V
1. Community Demographic Description (Cramer’s V=0.053) A very weak but statistically significant association. Entirely rural students show the highest graduation rate (60.9%) and the lowest rate of not attending (21.5%), while town/rural mix students show the lowest graduation rate (51.2%) and the highest rate of not attending (29.8%). Urban/town/rural mix students fall in between (55.1% graduated, 26.0% did not attend). Attended but did not graduate rates are nearly identical across all three community types (17-19%), suggesting that community type influences the decision to attend rather than completion once enrolled.
2. EDR (Cramer’s V=0.023) A negligible association. Graduation rates are nearly identical across EDR 6E Southwest Central (51.5%), EDR 6W Upper Minnesota Valley (52.9%), and EDR 8 Southwest (54.5%), as are rates of not attending (29.2%, 29.1%, and 26.7% respectively). Geographic subregion within Southwest Minnesota has virtually no bearing on postsecondary attendance outcomes.
Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared
1. Average Unemployment Rate (η²=0.007) A negligible effect size despite statistical significance. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests reveal that did not attend PS individuals experienced significantly lower average unemployment rates than both attended groups (estimate= -0.303 vs did not grad, p<0.001; estimate=-0.218 vs grad, p<0.001), consistent with the opportunity cost hypothesis — favorable local labor market conditions are associated with direct workforce entry rather than postsecondary attendance. The difference between the two attended groups is smaller but significant (estimate=-0.085, p<0.001).
2. Average Wages as Percentage of State Average (η²=0.006) A negligible effect size. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests show that graduates experience slightly higher relative wages than non-completers (estimate=0.004, p<0.001), while did not attend PS individuals experience significantly lower relative wages than both attended groups, consistent with the opportunity cost hypothesis but with a very small practical effect.
Summary High school characteristics and labor market variables show negligible to very weak associations with postsecondary attendance outcomes. EDR shows a negligible association, confirming that geographic subregion within Southwest Minnesota has virtually no bearing on postsecondary attendance decisions. Community demographic description shows a slightly stronger but still very weak association, with entirely rural students showing the highest graduation rates. Both labor market variables show associations consistent with the opportunity cost hypothesis but with negligible effect sizes, suggesting that local labor market conditions play a very modest role in postsecondary attendance decisions in this regional context.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
High School Accomplishments
Here are the variables we are exploring;

High school accomplishments

took.ACT
ACTCompositeScore
ap.exam
pseo.participant
cte.achievement
total.cte.courses.taken
MCA.M
MCA.R
MCA.S




Summary
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis and one-way ANOVA examined the relationship between high school accomplishment variables and postsecondary attendance outcomes (Did not attend PS, Attended - did not grad, Attended - grad) for 47,189 individuals, with the exception of pseo.participant which excludes the 2008-2010 graduation cohorts. Categorical variables are ranked by Cramer’s V and continuous variables by eta-squared.
Categorical Variables - Ranked by Cramer’s V
1. took.ACT (Cramer’s V=0.443) The strongest categorical predictor of postsecondary attendance. Students who took the ACT show a graduation rate of 67.3% compared to just 26.6% for non-ACT takers, while 53.6% of non-ACT students did not attend postsecondary compared to only 14.4% of ACT takers. This is the largest differential observed across any categorical variable examined for postsecondary attendance.
2. pseo.participant (Cramer’s V=0.401) The second strongest categorical predictor. PSEO participants show a graduation rate of 76.3% compared to 36.5% for non-participants, while 42.1% of non-participants did not attend postsecondary compared to only 10.8% of PSEO participants. The near-tautological nature of this relationship should be noted — PSEO participants are by definition already engaged in college coursework while in high school.
3. ap.exam (Cramer’s V=0.230) A moderate association. AP exam participants show a graduation rate of 79.4% — the highest of any categorical subgroup examined — and a very low rate of not attending (7.0%), while non-AP exam students show a lower graduation rate (48.6%) and higher rate of not attending (31.7%).
4. cte.achievement (Cramer’s V=0.041) A very weak association. Graduation rates show a modest inverse relationship with CTE engagement level — No CTE students show the highest graduation rate (56.4%), followed by CTE Participants (55.2%) and CTE Concentrators or Completors (50.6%). Higher CTE engagement is modestly associated with lower postsecondary graduation rates, consistent with the workforce pathway sorting mechanism identified throughout this analysis.
Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared
1. MCA Reading (η²=0.106) The strongest continuous predictor with a medium effect size. Graduates score significantly higher than non-completers (estimate=0.333, p<0.001), while did not attend PS individuals score significantly lower than both attended groups (estimate=-0.388 vs did not grad, p<0.001; estimate=-0.721 vs grad, p<0.001). The large gap between graduates and did not attend PS individuals confirms Reading achievement as the strongest continuous predictor of postsecondary pathway.
2. ACT Composite Score (η²=0.094) A small to medium effect size, restricted to ACT takers. Graduates show substantially higher ACT scores than non-completers (estimate=2.035, p<0.001), while did not attend PS individuals show significantly lower scores than both non-completers (estimate= -1.848, p<0.001) and graduates (estimate=-3.883, p<0.001). The very large gap between graduates and did not attend PS individuals reflects the powerful sorting role of academic achievement in postsecondary pathway selection.
3. MCA Science (η²=0.088) A small to medium effect size, noting the voluntary and self-selected nature of Science MCA participation. Graduates show significantly higher Science scores than non-completers (estimate=0.346, p<0.001) and did not attend PS individuals show significantly lower scores than both attended groups (estimate= -0.264 vs did not grad, p<0.001; estimate=-0.611 vs grad, p<0.001).
4. MCA Math (η²=0.079) A small to medium effect size mirroring the MCA Reading and Science patterns. Graduates score significantly higher than non-completers (estimate=0.422, p<0.001) and did not attend PS individuals score significantly lower than graduates (estimate=-0.668, p<0.001) and non-completers (estimate=-0.246, p<0.001).
5. Total CTE Courses Taken (η²=0.008) A negligible effect size despite statistical significance. Graduates show significantly fewer CTE courses than non-completers (estimate=-0.668, p<0.001), while no significant difference is found between did not attend PS and non-completers (p=0.526). Did not attend PS individuals show significantly more CTE courses than graduates (estimate=0.726, p<0.001), consistent with the inverse relationship between CTE engagement and postsecondary attendance identified throughout this analysis.
Summary High school accomplishment variables show the strongest associations with postsecondary attendance outcomes of any variable group examined. took.ACT, pseo.participant, and all three MCA scores show meaningful effect sizes, with MCA Reading emerging as the strongest continuous predictor (η²=0.106). A clear and consistent pattern emerges across both categorical and continuous variables — academic achievement and college preparedness indicators are strongly associated with postsecondary attendance and completion, while CTE engagement shows a modest inverse relationship. The negligible effect of total CTE courses taken on postsecondary attendance, combined with its meaningful association with regional workforce participation identified earlier, further reinforces the distinct and complementary nature of academic and CTE pathways in Southwest Minnesota.

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Summary
Cross-tabulation analysis and one-way ANOVA examined associations between independent variables and postsecondary graduation (College completer vs Non-college completer) for 47,189 individuals. Variables are presented in two separate ranked lists by effect size measure. Binary categorical variables report phi (φ) and multi-category variables report Cramer’s V.
Categorical Variables - Ranked by Effect Size

took.ACT (φ=0.389) — Joint strongest categorical predictor alongside pseo.participant. ACT takers show a completion rate of 67.3% compared to 26.6% for non-takers, with non-ACT takers showing a non-completion rate of 73.4%.
pseo.participant (φ=0.389) — Joint strongest categorical predictor. PSEO participants show a completion rate of 76.3% compared to 36.5% for non-participants, noting the near- tautological nature of this relationship.
economic.status (φ=0.289) — The strongest demographic predictor. Non-disadvantaged students show a completion rate of 64.4% compared to 34.6% for economically disadvantaged students, a gap of nearly 30 percentage points.
special.ed.status (φ=0.256) — The second strongest demographic predictor. Special education students show a completion rate of just 21.7% compared to 58.3% for non-special education students, the lowest completion rate of any demographic subgroup examined.
ap.exam (φ=0.220) — A moderate association. AP exam participants show the highest completion rate of any categorical subgroup examined (79.4%) compared to 48.6% for non-AP exam students.
race.ethnicity (Cramer’s V=0.200) — A moderate association. White students show the highest completion rate (59.0%) while AI students show the lowest (24.7%), with a non-completion rate of 75.3%.
english.learner (φ=0.193) — A moderate association. English learners show a completion rate of 24.0% compared to 56.3% for non-English learners, one of the largest completion rate differentials observed across all demographic variables.
non.english.home (φ=0.158) — A weak to moderate association closely mirroring english.learner. Students from non-English speaking homes show a completion rate of 28.9% compared to 55.7%.
gender (φ=0.126) — A weak association. Females show a notably higher completion rate (59.6% vs 47.0%), consistent with well-established national patterns of female postsecondary attainment advantage.
dem.desc (Cramer’s V=0.070) — A very weak association. Entirely rural students show the highest completion rate (60.9%) while town/rural mix students show the lowest (51.2%).
cte.achievement (Cramer’s V=0.052) — A very weak and inverse association. No CTE students show the highest completion rate (56.4%) while CTE Concentrators or Completors show the lowest (50.6%), consistent with the workforce pathway sorting mechanism.
edr (Cramer’s V=0.027) — A negligible association. Completion rates are nearly identical across all three EDRs, confirming that geographic subregion has virtually no bearing on postsecondary graduation outcomes.

Continuous Variables - Ranked by Eta-Squared

MCA Reading (η²=0.087) — The strongest continuous predictor with a small to medium effect. College completers score significantly higher than non-completers (estimate=0.555), confirming Reading achievement as the strongest MCA predictor of postsecondary graduation.
ACT Composite Score (η²=0.082) — A small to medium effect restricted to ACT takers. College completers show substantially higher ACT scores than non-completers (estimate=2.847), reflecting the strong relationship between academic achievement and postsecondary completion.
MCA Science (η²=0.078) — A small to medium effect, noting the voluntary and self-selected nature of participation. College completers score significantly higher than non-completers (estimate=0.501).
MCA Math (η²=0.073) — A small to medium effect mirroring Reading and Science. College completers score significantly higher than non-completers (estimate=0.563).
avg.wages.pct.state (η²=0.004) — A negligible effect. College completers experience slightly higher relative wages, consistent with the expectation that graduates enter higher-wage labor markets but with virtually no practical predictive value.
avg.unemployment.rate (η²=0.001) — A negligible effect. College completers experienced slightly higher local unemployment rates, consistent with the opportunity cost hypothesis but with essentially zero practical predictive value.
total.cte.courses.taken (η²=0.008) — A negligible effect. Non-completers show significantly more CTE courses than completers (estimate=0.703), consistent with the inverse relationship between CTE engagement and postsecondary completion observed throughout this analysis.

Overall Pattern The predictors of postsecondary graduation closely mirror those of postsecondary attendance, with academic achievement and college preparedness variables dominating and demographic disadvantage variables showing moderate to strong associations. The most notable finding unique to postsecondary graduation is that MCA Reading emerges as the strongest continuous predictor, suggesting reading proficiency is particularly important for credential completion beyond mere enrollment. Geographic variables continue to show negligible associations, confirming that individual-level academic preparation and demographic characteristics — not geographic origin within Southwest Minnesota — are the primary drivers of postsecondary graduation outcomes. The consistent inverse relationship between CTE engagement and postsecondary graduation across both CTE achievement and total CTE courses taken reinforces the distinct and complementary nature of academic and CTE pathways throughout this analysis.

data <- master.original %>%
  filter(ps.grad != "Attending ps") %>%
  mutate(ps.grad = ifelse(ps.grad == "Y", "College completer", "Non-college completer"))

title: Post-secondary pathways
text:
Demographics

Summary
Overview Cross-tabulation analysis examined the relationship between demographic variables and postsecondary graduation (College completer vs Non-college completer) for 47,189 individuals. Binary variables report phi (φ), which is equivalent to Cramer’s V for 2x2 tables. Variables are ranked by effect size from strongest to weakest association.
Ranked by Effect Size:
1. Economic Status (φ=0.289) The strongest demographic predictor of postsecondary graduation. Non-disadvantaged students show a completion rate of 64.4% compared to just 34.6% for economically disadvantaged students — a gap of nearly 30 percentage points. Economic disadvantage is clearly the most powerful demographic barrier to postsecondary completion in this dataset.
2. Special Education Status (φ=0.256) The second strongest demographic predictor. Special education students show a dramatically lower completion rate (21.7% vs 58.3%), the lowest of any demographic subgroup examined. The nearly 37 percentage point gap between special education and non-special education students is the largest differential observed across any binary demographic variable.
3. Race/Ethnicity (Cramer’s V=0.200) A moderate association with notable variation across groups. White students show the highest completion rate (59.0%), followed by Asian/PI (42.9%) and Black (39.5%) students. AI (24.7%) and Hispanic (26.5%) students show the lowest completion rates, with AI students showing a non-completion rate of 75.3% — the highest of any racial/ethnic group. Small sample sizes for minority groups warrant cautious interpretation.
4. English Learner Status (φ=0.193) A moderate association. English learners show a completion rate of just 24.0% compared to 56.3% for non-English learners, a gap of over 32 percentage points. This is one of the largest completion rate differentials observed across all demographic variables, suggesting English learner status is a substantial barrier to postsecondary completion.
5. Non-English Home Language (φ=0.158) A weak to moderate association closely mirroring the English learner pattern. Students from non-English speaking homes show a completion rate of 28.9% compared to 55.7%, a gap of nearly 27 percentage points.
6. Gender (φ=0.126) The weakest demographic predictor. Females show a notably higher completion rate (59.6% vs 47.0%), a gap of nearly 13 percentage points. This gender gap in postsecondary completion is consistent with well-established national patterns and is one of the more substantively important findings given its implications for regional workforce development.
Summary Economic status and special education status are the strongest demographic predictors of postsecondary graduation, both showing gaps of over 30 percentage points between advantaged and disadvantaged groups. Race/ethnicity shows a moderate association with particularly low completion rates for AI and Hispanic students. English learner status and non-English home language show moderate associations with large completion rate differentials, suggesting language barriers are a significant obstacle to postsecondary completion. Gender shows the weakest association but in a substantively important direction, with females showing considerably higher completion rates than males across all groups examined.
